ci: Stories are shared in different ways.
lo: Imagining stories together; Ways stories are shared; Connecting with stories

s.d: Students perform their original story in front of the green screen, transporting themselves inside the settings they imagined — a rainforest, a castle, outer space — with each student taking a role as narrator, character or sound effects creator, combining live performance with digital environments they chose from the mystery box costumes provocation. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code a 3-4 page animated retelling of their story in Scratch Jr where characters walk, talk, grow, shrink and change backgrounds between scenes. The characters move and speak on their own when you press the green flag — the story performs itself. This is fundamentally different from a book or video because the student built a machine that tells the story autonomously.

Students create a 30-second soundscape for their story using only sound effects and music (no words), then play it for a partner who has to guess what the story is about. This explores a completely different way stories are shared — through sound alone — and couldn't happen without audio layering tools. Connects directly to the central idea that stories are shared in different ways.

Students create an interactive 'Story Museum' book where each page is a different room showcasing a different way of sharing stories explored during the unit — one page has an embedded video of their puppet show, another has a voice-recorded oral retelling, another has photos of their playdough scene with labels — so the book itself demonstrates the central idea that stories are shared in different ways.

Students contribute to a class Padlet board where each child posts a photo of their favourite story moment from the unit alongside a voice recording retelling what happened — building a collaborative 'Story Wall' the class can revisit and discuss.

ci: Everyone has a role to play in the community.
lo: My communities; Contributing to the local community; Connections in my community

s.d: Students dress up as different school community members (teacher, librarian, office staff, grounds crew) and film themselves in front of the green screen placed in the actual school locations where those people work — the library, the front office, the garden. Each student explains what their community role does and why it matters, creating a 'Meet Our School Heroes' video that could be played at the next school assembly or shown to new Prep families. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students produce a 'Community Heroes' news report — filming interviews with real school staff members (the librarian, office manager, grounds crew) asking 'What is your role and why does it matter?', then editing the clips together with a student anchor introducing each segment. The finished news report is played at assembly, showcasing the community helpers they investigated during the Week 1 photo walk.

Students code a Scratch Jr animation showing 'A Day in Our Community' where different characters (teacher, crossing guard, librarian, parent helper) appear on screen and perform their role — the teacher opens a book, the crossing guard stops traffic, the librarian shelves books. When all characters have performed, a final screen says 'Everyone has a role to play!' — the central idea brought to life through code.

Students explore the streets around Elsternwick on Google Maps Street View, spotting community helpers and services (the fire station, the library, shops, the school). They take screenshots of places they recognise and the teacher builds a class collection on the board — discovering that their community is full of people playing roles they might not have noticed before.

Students program Beebots to visit different 'community role' stations on a class-made mat — post office, fire station, school, hospital, and shops — explaining at each stop what the people there do and how they help the community. Before programming, each pair plans the most logical route between stations, discovering that the order of visits matters and that their sequence tells a story about how community services connect. The planning and debugging process mirrors the unit's central idea that communities organise roles and systems to help people live and work together.

ci: Scientists follow a method to discover new things.
lo: The scientific process; Observing my world; Drawing conclusions

s.d: Students produce a 'Junior Scientists' documentary where each group films themselves conducting one experiment from the unit (the ice cube melting test, the ramp surface test, the Nature Detective Walk observations) — using slow-motion to capture the moment of change, adding narration explaining their prediction vs what actually happened, and delivering a 'scientist's conclusion' to camera. The class watches all documentaries together as a mini film festival. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students code a simple Scratch Jr sequence that mirrors the scientific method they followed — a character walks to the experiment, a question mark appears (What will happen?), the character performs the test, then a tick or cross appears showing what they discovered. Each step in the code represents a step in the process: observe, predict, test, conclude. Programming the method as a sequence helps students understand that science — like code — follows deliberate steps in a specific order to reach a reliable result.

Students create a 'Sounds of Science' audio guide for the class science display — recording narrated explanations of each experiment with sound effects (bubbling for the melting test, rolling sounds for the ramp, bird calls for the Nature Walk). The audio guide is played through a speaker as visitors explore the display, transforming a static poster exhibition into an immersive listening experience that couldn't exist without audio production tools.

Students create a 'My Science Book' in Book Creator with four pages — one for each step of the scientific method they practised: an Observe page (a photo or drawing of what they noticed), a Predict page (a voice recording of their guess), a Test page (a photo of themselves conducting the experiment), and a Conclude page (a voice recording of what they found out). The four-page structure mirrors the sequential logic of scientific thinking, and the finished book is a tangible artefact that proves the student followed the method — not just watched it happen. Books are shared in a mini 'Science Showcase' where pairs read each other's pages and compare whether their conclusions matched their predictions.

Students photograph their science experiments and use Seesaw drawing tools to circle what they observed changing, recording a voice note explaining what happened when they tested different materials — building scientific documentation skills throughout the unit.

ci: Living things share our world and have an important role in nature
lo: The different living things around us; The roles living things play in nature; How humans and other living things share space

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Discovering our past helps us know more about ourselves today.
lo: Our personal stories and histories; Reflecting on and recording our past; Our ancestry and families

s.d: Planner content not yet uploaded — specific suggestions pending. Please send the UOI planner for this unit to Nathan Benn on Teams to generate tailored technology integration suggestions.

ci: Relationships impact our wellbeing.
lo: Developing relationships; How those around us impact our behaviours and interactions; Keeping ourselves healthy and safe

s.d: Students perform their 'friendship rescue' scenario in front of the green screen, but the backdrop is set to a real photograph of the actual school space where the scenario takes place — the classroom corner, the playground equipment, the lunch area — rather than a generic digital location. Using a photo of the real place makes the relationship strategies feel immediately applicable: the kindness being demonstrated happens right there, in a spot every student recognises. The class watches all performances and discusses which strategies worked, pointing to the familiar locations visible behind their peers. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students use Scratch Jr to code one simple action showing a positive relationship behaviour — a character moves across the screen to share space with another, waves hello, or changes expression from sad to happy when someone approaches kindly. Students choose just one behaviour and build it using two or three blocks. The simplicity is intentional: Prep students achieve a complete coding success, building confidence through a short, achievable task that makes one kind action visible and repeatable. When the green flag is pressed, their character is kind on its own — the student made that happen through code.

Students film a 'Wellbeing News Report' where a student reporter interviews classmates about the relationship strategies that worked best during the unit — filmed around the school in the actual locations where friendships happen (playground, classroom, library). The reporter adds a verdict at the end: 'The number one thing that helps relationships is...' with slow-motion replays of friendship in action during PE cooperative activities. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students program Beebots to travel a 'Friendship Path' floor mat — a grid of A3 cards placed on the classroom floor, each illustrated with one positive relationship behaviour: Sharing, Listening, Including, Helping, and Kind Words. Before programming, pairs plan their route using physical coding blocks, counting steps and turns to reach each station in their chosen order. When the Beebot arrives at a card, students explain what that behaviour looks like in real life and why it matters. The floor grid makes social-emotional vocabulary physical and spatial: students move through the language of healthy relationships rather than just talking about it, building a concrete map of the behaviours explored throughout the unit.

After each PE cooperative games session — where students practise sharing equipment, joining in with new friends, and helping others — students open Seesaw and photograph a moment from the session that shows a positive relationship behaviour in action. Using the drawing tool they circle the behaviour they can see, then record a voice note answering one simple question: 'Who did you help or include today, and how?' The PE photograph anchors the reflection in something real and immediate rather than abstract memory, making the task genuinely Prep-accessible. Teachers build a growing visual archive of cooperation evidence that tracks the unit's social-emotional learning through observable, documented moments of students being kind in action.

ci: Emotions can be expressed in different ways.
lo: Recognising our own and others' emotions; Responding to and interpreting our emotions; Different ways we express emotions

s.d: Students perform their emotion masks in front of the green screen — each child holds up the mask they created and acts out the emotion in an environment that matches (the angry mask in a thunderstorm, the calm mask on a beach, the excited mask at a party). The green screen transforms their single mask into a full emotional scene, demonstrating that emotions are expressed through body language, colour, setting AND facial expression simultaneously. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code an 'Emotion Charades' game in Scratch Jr — a character's face and body change to show an emotion (jumping for excited, drooping for sad, shaking for angry, floating for calm), and the audience must guess which emotion is being expressed. Each student codes a different emotion, and the class plays each other's games — learning to read non-verbal expression cues through an interactive coded experience.

Students create a short soundscape to accompany an emotion (happy, sad, angry, calm), connecting to the music lessons where they explored expressing emotions through sound and created soundscapes to accompany different feelings.

Students post photos of their colour-themed emotion artworks onto a class Padlet gallery, adding voice notes explaining which emotion they expressed and why they chose those colours and shapes — building a collaborative 'Feelings Exhibition' wall.

Students record a Seesaw video of themselves demonstrating one emotion through three different expression methods — first through body and facial expression (drama), then through a GarageBand sound they created, then through a drawing. Playing all three back, they explain which method communicated the emotion most clearly and why — building the metacognitive vocabulary (expression, medium, communicate) the unit develops while creating a multimodal portfolio that captures the full arc of the inquiry.

ci: Services are established to help the needs of people in the community.
lo: Local services; Why we need local services in our community; How services meet personal and community needs

s.d: Students produce a 'Community Service Spotlight' video — filming a real thank-you message to the community worker they wrote their appreciation letter for, then walking to the service location (library, fire station, local shop) to film an on-location report explaining what the service does and why the community needs it. The finished video is shared at assembly as a genuine act of appreciation.

Students code a 'Community Service Simulator' in Scratch Jr where a character visits different services throughout their day — going to the doctor when sick, visiting the library for a book, calling the fire brigade for an emergency. Each scene shows the service in action with characters moving and interacting, demonstrating how services meet different needs at different times — a coded model of community systems.

Students film themselves as community service workers in front of the green screen — placed inside a fire station, hospital, library, or school office. Each student explains their role, demonstrates what they do in an emergency or typical day, and explains why the community needs them — connecting to their Learner Profile focus on caring and the assembly presentation about community. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students post photos and drawings of community services they discovered during their local area investigation, adding voice notes explaining what each service does and why the community needs it — building a collaborative map of how services connect.

Unlike the Prep activity (which finds and names local services), Year 1 students use Google Maps Street View to investigate how services CONNECT to each other. Pairs trace the route a community member would need to take during a challenging day — from home to the doctor, to the pharmacy, past the community centre, to the school — identifying each service along the route and discussing what specific need it meets at that moment. The question shifts from 'what is this service?' to 'how do these services work together to help someone through a hard day?' — building the Year 1 understanding that community services form an interconnected system, not a list of separate places.

ci: Patterns help us to interpret the world around us.
lo: Patterns and cycles in our lives; Different patterns around us; How patterns can be created, interpreted and extended

s.d: Students code a life cycle animation in Scratch Jr showing the stages of the organism they researched on Epic — butterfly egg to caterpillar to chrysalis to butterfly, with each stage as a new scene where the character transforms. The animation loops automatically, demonstrating visually that life cycles ARE patterns that repeat — connecting coding loops to the biological concept explored throughout the unit.

Students produce a 'Pattern Hunters' documentary — filming three different types of patterns found around the school. Two are visual patterns captured in close-up: repeating shapes in the built environment (bricks, tiles, window grids) and repeating structures in nature (leaf veining, petal arrangements). The third must be a MOVEMENT pattern filmed in slow motion — a skipping rope arc, a pendulum swing, or a Beebot's repeating turn sequence — where slow motion is genuinely necessary to reveal the repeating structure to the eye. Students explain what would happen if each pattern broke. The combination of close-up for still patterns and slow motion for movement patterns builds the key understanding that patterns exist in both space and time. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students program Beebots to travel a repeating pattern route — forward, turn, forward, turn — tracing geometric shapes on a large grid mat. They design their pattern sequence first on paper, then enter it into the Beebot and observe whether the robot follows their predicted path. When it doesn't, they debug and adjust, discovering that patterns in code — like patterns in nature — follow precise, repeatable rules. The physical movement of the robot makes the abstract concept of pattern sequencing concrete and testable.

Students program micro:bits to display repeating pattern sequences on the LED screen (dot patterns cycling through shapes), comparing digital patterns with the natural patterns they discovered in living things — connecting coding logic to the mathematical patterns observed in nature.

Students use Seesaw to create a 'Pattern Spotter' record — photographing three patterns they find in the classroom, school garden, or their own clothing (stripes, spots, repeating shapes, colour sequences) and recording a voice note identifying the rule that makes each pattern repeat. Collecting patterns from the real environment and articulating the underlying rule builds the unit's key understanding: that patterns have structure, and recognising that structure is how we interpret and extend them — a skill that transfers to music, maths, coding and science.

ci: Plants are a life sustaining resource.
lo: Products derived from plants; Plants all around us; The interconnectedness of plants

s.d: Planner content not yet uploaded — specific suggestions pending. Please send the UOI planner for this unit to Nathan Benn on Teams to generate tailored technology integration suggestions.

Students use micro:bit light and temperature sensors to collect data in the school garden environment, monitoring conditions that support the living things they are studying and connecting sensor readings to discussions about how sharing the planet means understanding what other species need.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Homes reflect our culture and environment.
lo: Different types of homes; Characteristics of a home; The importance of sustainable living spaces

s.d: Students film their mini home building project presentation using Canva Video with the green screen feature active — replacing the background with a photograph of the actual geographic environment the home was designed for (Arctic tundra for the igloo, Australian outback for the gunya hut, Mongolian steppe for the ger). They explain why their home looks the way it does, connecting materials and design to climate and culture. Using Canva rather than a standalone green screen setup means filming, background replacement, and titling all happen in one accessible platform — and the finished video is ready to share with families without additional editing steps.

Students produce a 'Homes Around the World' travel show — each group films a segment introducing their researched home type (igloo, houseboat, gunya hut, apartment), using their mini home as a prop and demonstrating how the design solves a problem (igloos trap warmth, houseboats float on water, gunya huts use available materials). Stop-motion reveals how the mini home was built step by step. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students code a 'Build-a-Home' animation in Scratch Jr showing a home being constructed piece by piece in its environment — blocks of ice stacking into an igloo against an Arctic backdrop, or sticks and bark forming a gunya hut in the Australian bush. The animation demonstrates the connection between materials, environment and culture through a coded construction sequence that plays automatically.

Students find their home on Google Maps, screenshot the Street View, and post it to Seesaw with a voice recording explaining what they notice about the place where they live — the materials their home is made from, what makes it suited to Elsternwick's environment, and how it compares to the diverse home types studied during the unit (Arctic igloos, Mongolian gers, Japanese machiya). The activity personalises the unit's central idea that homes reflect culture and environment by starting with the student's own front door.

Students use Google Earth's satellite and 3D globe view — not Street View, which shows existing modern streets — to explore the actual geographic landscapes where the homes they studied were built: flying into the Arctic to see the treeless tundra where igloos were made from snow and ice, zooming into outback Australia for the sparse scrubland of gunya hut Country, and navigating the Mongolian steppe for ger territory. The satellite perspective reveals how geography determines building materials directly: there are no trees in the tundra, no ice blocks in the desert. The landscape is the building guide, and Google Earth makes that cause-and-effect relationship visible and explorable. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

Students explore the 'Historical Homes' build challenge — which takes players through ancient, medieval and modern architecture — to discover how homes across history reflect the culture and environment of the people who built them. They compare the home types encountered in Minecraft with the home types they researched (igloos, gunya huts, gers), identifying which environmental factors drove each design decision. Access at education.minecraft.net/challenges/historical-homes

ci: The actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing.
lo: Safe environments; How our actions affect ourselves and others; Keeping our body and mind healthy

s.d: Students produce a 'Safety Smart' news report — filming segments around the school showing safe vs unsafe actions in different environments (classroom, playground, swimming pool from the Intensive Swimming Program). Student reporters demonstrate the safe choice, then show the consequence of the unsafe choice in split-screen or slow-motion replay, delivering a verdict backed by what they learned about body parts and physical safety. This task works equally well in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express Video — use whichever platform the class is most familiar with, as all three support the split-screen, title card, and slow-motion format this news-report structure needs.

Students code a 'Choose the Safe Action' interactive animation in Scratch Jr — a character faces a scenario (crossing the road, using scissors, climbing the playground) and the player taps to choose the safe or unsafe response. Each choice leads to a different animated outcome showing the consequence, creating a cause-and-effect safety simulator that runs on code the student built.

Students create a short Canva Video demonstrating three physical wellbeing activities from their PE lessons — filming themselves performing each exercise and adding text title cards with simple instructions (e.g., 'Stand on one foot for 10 seconds', 'Take 5 slow deep breaths'). The finished video is played during actual class brain breaks and wellbeing moments, giving the students' work a real and recurring purpose beyond the unit. Canva's accessible editing tools mean Year 1 students can build the video independently, and the visual demonstration format makes the activities far easier to follow than an audio guide alone.

Students post photos of safe and unsafe examples they spot around the school onto a class Padlet board, adding voice notes explaining why each is safe or unsafe — building a collaborative reference wall that the class uses to develop their understanding of safe environments.

Students use Google Earth to explore the diverse environments that the living things studied during the unit call home — navigating from the Elsternwick school grounds to Arctic tundra, tropical rainforest, desert, and ocean. At each environment they identify the features that make it liveable for specific species, connecting the unit's inquiry into safe environments to the real geography of where those living things exist. At SKR where Pico VR is available, this activity can alternatively be delivered as a fully immersive habitat exploration — students step directly into each environment rather than navigating it from a screen. Note: this uses Google Earth's satellite and 3D globe view — not Street View. Students navigate the globe from above to explore landscapes and locations at scale.

ci: Artistic appreciation and expressions inspire our imagination and creativity.
lo: Forms of expression; Expressing thoughts and ideas; Understanding and appreciating art

s.d: Students produce an iMovie 'Art Gallery Opening' film where they walk viewers through their pet portrait artwork and creative process, using picture-in-picture to show their initial 'How am I creative?' self-reflection drawings beside the finished pieces — narrating their growth as risk-takers who embraced experimentation without fear of failure. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students code an interactive 'Creativity Museum' in Scratch Jr where visitors tap on different room sprites (visual art, music, drama, Lego, recycled materials) to trigger animations showing the creative process for each form of expression explored during the unit — demonstrating how artistic appreciation inspires imagination through an interactive format impossible without coding.

Students create short soundscapes in GarageBand to accompany their art gallery exhibition, composing music that reflects the mood and style of the artistic expressions explored throughout the unit including their pet portrait artwork and creative process documentation.

Students create an 'Art Exploration' portfolio documenting the different forms of expression explored during the unit — including their 'How am I creative?' self-reflection drawings, Lego building without a plan, dance and drama pieces, and their pet portrait combining various art styles and skills.

Students photograph their creative process during art activities (Lego building, collage making, drawing) and record reflections about how expression makes them feel, connecting to the Learner Profile focus on being a risk-taker who 'embraces experimentation'.

ci: Marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities.
lo: Needs and wants; The changing needs of communities; The design process

s.d: Students build a Lego Spike prototype of their marketplace product that includes a motorised or sensor-based feature (e.g. an automatic dispensing mechanism), programming it to demonstrate how their design meets the community need identified during the Sacred Heart Mission Glen Huntly Road excursion — presenting at a class marketplace exhibition.

Students produce an iMovie 'Shark Tank Junior' pitch video for their marketplace product, filming the design thinking journey (empathise, define, ideate, prototype, test) with time-lapse of their Maker Space creation process — delivering a persuasive pitch that demonstrates the difference between needs and wants learned from the sorting game provocation. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students code a simple two-scene animation in Scratch Jr showing what happens when the design process works (need → idea → prototype → solution) vs when steps are skipped (need → random guess → failure) — exploring the concept before the Sacred Heart Mission excursion deepens their understanding of community needs.

Students post their product designs at each stage of the design thinking process on a shared Padlet board, peer-reviewing each other's solutions and providing feedback on how well each product meets community needs identified during the Sacred Heart Mission excursion.

Students create a 'Community Services Guide' in Book Creator — one page per service investigated during the unit (doctor, fire station, library, post office, café), with a photo, a voice recording explaining what the service provides, and a drawing of who uses it and why. The completed guide becomes a real reference document for the classroom, giving the unit's key learning a genuine audience and purpose — demonstrating that services exist because people in a community have needs that require organised responses.

ci: Forces and motion are all around us.
lo: Forces around us; Scientific laws of motion; Innovations in technology

s.d: Students build a Lego Spike 'Force Testing Rig' that uses the motor to apply consistent push force and the distance sensor to measure how far objects travel across different surfaces — programming automated test runs, recording results, and presenting ranked friction data alongside their ramp experiment findings from the Maker Space.

Students produce an iMovie 'Forces Lab' episode filming their best Maker Space experiment (ramps, magnets, or simple machines) with slow-motion replay of the key moment, scientific vocabulary labels overlaid on screen, and a conclusion delivered 'sounding like scientists' — using the class vocab wall terms for prediction, method and conclusion. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students code an interactive 'Inventions Through the Ages' simulation in Scratch where clicking on simple machine sprites (lever, ramp, pulley, wheel) triggers animations showing how forces make them work, then reveals the modern invention they evolved into — connecting the Twisted Science excursion provocation to a digital museum visitors can explore.

Students use micro:bit accelerometers to measure the force of pushes during their ramp experiments, displaying readings on the LED screen and comparing how different forces and materials affect motion — connecting digital measurement to the scientific vocabulary from the class word wall.

Students produce a Canva Video science report presenting their forces and motion investigation — filming themselves demonstrating push, pull and gravity experiments, then narrating their findings with titles and annotations. Each group tests a different surface or ramp angle and presents their results as a short documentary, connecting their evidence to Newton's laws of motion explored during the unit. The video format requires students to explain their reasoning out loud, consolidating scientific vocabulary and the inquiry process.

ci: Taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make.
lo: Earth's resources; How our actions affect the environment; Ways we can care for our planet

s.d: Students program Beebots to navigate a 'Sustainable Choices' floor map — moving through green zones (responsible choices: recycling station, solar panels, community garden) while avoiding red zones (wasteful choices: single-use plastics, polluted waterways, deforestation). Students must plan their route before programming, deciding which sequence of responsible actions leads to a healthy planet. The physical act of guiding the robot through choices makes abstract environmental responsibility tangible and sequential — connecting directly to the unit's inquiry into how our actions affect the Earth.

Students produce an iMovie 'Earth Care News Report' filming their school environment with narration about responsible and irresponsible resource use they documented throughout the unit, using split-screen to show 'what we do now' vs 'what we should do' — building on the persuasive speech skills and Athletics unit transdisciplinary thinking. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students build a micro:bit 'Planet Care Dashboard' that uses the light sensor to monitor classroom energy use and displays a happy or sad face depending on whether lights are being used responsibly — presenting their working environmental monitor alongside their action plan for how the school community can make better choices for the planet.

Students photograph examples of resource use around school and draw/annotate how each choice helps or harms the environment, building understanding of the connection between daily actions and planetary wellbeing — documenting evidence for their persuasive speeches.

Students use Google Earth to explore the ecosystems connected to the living things they studied — zooming into the Great Barrier Reef, the Amazon rainforest, Australian wetlands, and urban green spaces. At each location they compare satellite imagery of healthy versus degraded versions of that ecosystem, gathering visual evidence for the unit's inquiry into actions that benefit or harm ecosystems. The satellite perspective makes global environmental responsibility feel tangible: students can zoom from Elsternwick's own parklands to ecosystems under threat on the same tool. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history.
lo: The significance of places to different people and cultures; The connection between landmarks and identity; How landmarks and symbols represent our history

s.d: Students produce an iMovie 'Landmarks of Elsternwick' documentary combining walking excursion footage (MLC, Wesley, Cato Park, Prest Room artefacts) with narration explaining what each landmark tells us about the community's history — using transitions, titles and picture-in-picture to layer historical photos alongside present-day footage. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students film a green screen time-travel broadcast where they 'visit' Elsternwick landmarks in their historical context using old photos as backdrops, reporting on the significance of each place to different people and cultures — connecting to the Possum Magic capital cities mapping and the Year 2 Purple Acknowledgement of Country. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code an interactive 'Elsternwick Map Tour' in Scratch Jr where tapping landmark sprites triggers animations and recorded voice explanations about each place's significance, how symbols represent landmarks, and connections between map-reading skills from Possum Magic and understanding significance of place for different cultures.

Students explore the streets around Elsternwick on Google Maps Street View — finding landmarks from the unit (the Astor Theatre, Rippon Lea, St Columbas) in their actual street context. They screenshot their finds and post to Seesaw with voice annotations explaining the landmark's significance and what story it tells about Elsternwick's history. Walking the streets virtually before visiting in person gives students geographic anchoring and sharpens their observation skills for the excursion.

Students use Google Earth to explore Elsternwick's landmarks from above and at street level — navigating to Rippon Lea Estate, the Astor Theatre, Balaclava's cultural precinct, and St Kilda Road's historical buildings. They compare the satellite view with historical photographs of the same sites, identifying what survives from the past and what has changed. The virtual exploration builds geographical familiarity with the unit's key locations before excursion visits, so students arrive knowing what to look for and carrying specific historical questions. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

ci: Our relationships with others impact our lives.
lo: Importance of relationships; Developing and maintaining healthy relationships; The impact of relationships on wellbeing

s.d: Students film a green screen 'Relationship Report' where they transport themselves to different social settings (playground, classroom, sports field, home) and perform scenarios demonstrating healthy relationship strategies — connecting the Padlet wonderwall examples to a broadcast format that brings the strategies to life in impossible locations.

Students compose a 'Relationships Soundscape' in GarageBand layering sounds that represent different relationship emotions explored during the unit — cooperation (harmonious instruments), conflict (clashing beats), resolution (melody returning) — performing their composition live while classmates guess which relationship scenario each section represents, connecting to PE teamwork reflections.

Students code an interactive 'Friendship Scenario' in Scratch Jr where animated characters face a relationship challenge (based on PE cooperation activities) and the viewer taps to choose a response — each choice triggers different consequences, showing how open-mindedness and balanced approaches affect wellbeing outcomes.

Students design a short Microsoft Forms survey asking three questions about healthy relationships — 'What makes you feel safe with a friend?', 'What would you do if a friendship felt unkind?', 'What is one thing a good friend does?' — and collect responses anonymously from their class. Analysing the results together, they look for patterns in what their peers value in relationships, building a class-generated evidence base for the unit's central idea that our relationships with others impact our lives in ways both positive and challenging.

Students draw themselves and record a voice note answering 'What does a healthy relationship look like?' — creating a personal snapshot that the class revisits at the end of the unit to see how their understanding of relationships has grown.

ci: Celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community.
lo: The ways personal and cultural events are celebrated; Our appreciation of the diversity of cultures; How celebrations shape community identity

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Cultural Celebrations Museum' in Scratch where visitors click on celebration sprites (Chinese New Year, Diwali, Easter, NAIDOC) to trigger animations, music samples from their GarageBand compositions, and recorded voice explanations — creating a digital exhibition that celebrates the diversity of cultures and how celebrations shape community identity.

Students produce a short documentary about a celebration or tradition important to their family or culture, including interviews with family members and connections to the music provocation exploring Gurrumal's 'I Am Australian' and what it means to belong to a diverse Australian community.

Students build a 3D 'Celebrations Museum' in CoSpaces — creating walkable exhibition rooms for each cultural celebration studied (Chinese New Year, Diwali, Easter, NAIDOC) with clickable objects that reveal the traditions, foods, symbols and community significance of each event. Other classes and parents can be invited to explore the virtual museum, giving the student research a real-world audience. The interactive format demonstrates how celebrations shape community identity by asking visitors to walk through and experience the cultural richness students have curated.

Students create a cultural research book documenting different celebrations and traditions explored during the unit, including representations of what the colours and symbols of the Australian flag represent, and personal family stories about how cultural events are celebrated.

Students choose a character in Adobe Express Character Animator to represent a cultural celebration they researched — dressing the scene with a culturally appropriate background and adding music. They record their voice as a community member explaining why the celebration matters to their culture and how it shapes their identity. The animated performance combines the unit's music, drama and inquiry strands into a single digital artefact — and the class collection becomes a multicultural showcase that celebrates the diversity of communities explored throughout the unit.

ci: The rights and responsibilities of citizens are determined by the rules and laws of a community.
lo: The differences between rules and laws; People responsible for enforcing rules and laws; Rights and responsibilities of citizens; The consequences when rules, laws and rights are not met

s.d: Students build an interactive 3D courtroom in CoSpaces where visitors walk through scenarios involving rules vs laws — clicking on characters triggers explanations of who enforces the rule or law, what the consequence is, and how citizens' rights and responsibilities connect — creating an immersive experience that brings the card game provocation to life in a virtual world.

Students produce a podcast debate episode structured around a real community dilemma where rules and laws collide — for example: 'Should a park curfew law override a community group's right to gather?' Each pair of students takes one side, presents their argument using evidence from the unit's research into rights, responsibilities, and consequences, then responds to their partner's counterargument. The teacher compiles the best episodes into a class 'Citizens' Debate' series. The debate format operationalises the unit's central idea directly: students must decide which laws and rights take precedence, who enforces them, and what the consequences of each position would be — the same judgements citizens and lawmakers must make in practice. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students design and code a Scratch game where the player takes on the role of a community law enforcement officer responding to different scenarios — a shopkeeper reports stolen goods, a neighbour complains about loud noise, a cyclist rides without a helmet. For each scenario, the player must choose the correct response (warning, fine, referral to court) based on whether the situation involves a broken rule or a broken law, earning points for correct decisions and receiving feedback explaining the consequences when rules and laws are not met.

Students collaboratively create quiz questions about rules vs laws, rights vs responsibilities, and the role of different people in enforcing them — used as a review tool connecting to the Principled Learner Profile focus on accepting consequences of actions.

Students use Freeform to collaboratively map how rules and laws work as interconnected systems — plotting the differences between rules and laws, who enforces them, and how rights and responsibilities connect, building a visual concept map throughout the unit.

ci: Humans use their understanding of scientific principles to take action.
lo: The work of scientists; The process of learning; How science brings about new understandings and changes

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Science Principles in Action' simulation in Scratch where clicking on different experiment sprites triggers animated demonstrations of the scientific principles investigated during the unit, with data overlays from their micro:bit sensor investigations — creating a digital science museum visitors can explore.

Students design a 3D model in Tinkercad of a scientific instrument or tool invented by the scientist they researched, with movable parts showing how the scientific principle works — presenting their digital design alongside their research profile explaining how this scientist's discoveries changed the world.

Students produce a short documentary about their chosen scientist's impact on the world, incorporating research from guided and shared reading experiences, class videos of scientists, and discussions about how discoveries change society.

Students build a Lego Spike mechanism demonstrating a scientific principle their chosen scientist discovered, programming the motor and sensors to create a working model that shows cause and effect, presenting the prototype alongside their research poster and documentary.

Students collaboratively build a Kahoot quiz about the scientific principles and scientists studied during the unit — each group is responsible for three questions about their assigned scientist's discovery, the principle it demonstrated, and how it changed scientific understanding. Playing the quiz tests the whole class's breadth of knowledge across multiple scientists and principles, and the discussion after each wrong answer deepens understanding. Building the quiz requires students to identify what is genuinely important to know — a higher-order task than answering questions.

ci: Adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments.
lo: Adaptations and structural features of living things; Roles of living things in an ecosystem; Actions that benefit or harm ecosystems

s.d: Students conduct a school ecosystem impact audit — using Microsoft Forms to tally lunchbox waste by category (plastic, glass, paper, organic, metal) across multiple classes. The auto-generated Forms charts make visible which human actions — packaging choices, single-use plastics, food waste — are creating the most ecosystem harm. Students post their charts to a shared Padlet, compare results across classes, and connect the data directly to the unit's third line of inquiry: actions that benefit or harm ecosystems. The audit transforms an abstract ecological concept into student-generated evidence, and the reduction proposal becomes a genuine act of environmental stewardship.

Students produce a 'Planet Protectors' podcast episode discussing actions that benefit or harm ecosystems, featuring their research on animal adaptations and sustainability, and proposing actions their school community could take to protect local ecosystems. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students create an animal adaptation research book documenting the species they investigated, explaining physical and behavioural adaptations, the animal's role in its ecosystem, and connecting to observations from the Melbourne Aquarium excursion about how living things are adapted to their environments.

Students build a functioning ecosystem in Minecraft Education — recreating the specific habitat of the animal they researched (reef, rainforest, wetland, grassland) with accurate structural features, food sources, and shelter types based on their investigation and Melbourne Aquarium excursion observations. They then introduce a human impact (pollution, deforestation, habitat clearing) and observe and document how the ecosystem changes — which species lose food or shelter first, how the change cascades through the food web. The Minecraft ecosystem makes the unit's central idea visible and testable: students can see adaptation and survival not as abstract concepts but as spatial and relational problems that their chosen animal must solve within a world students built and understand completely.

Students film themselves 'reporting live' from an ecosystem (using green screen), presenting their research on how adaptations ensure survival, incorporating findings from the Melbourne Aquarium excursion and class discussions about the positive and negative impact humans have on habitats.

Students explore the 'We Are the Rangers' world — developed with United for Wildlife — where they play as conservation rangers protecting threatened species in authentic habitats. They investigate how adaptations help animals survive, map food web relationships, and design in-game interventions to address threats like habitat loss. The experience makes the unit's central idea — that adaptation and sustainability ensure survival — something students inhabit rather than just study. Access the world and lesson plans at education.minecraft.net/resources/climate-science/we-are-the-rangers

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society.
lo: The factors that motivate exploration (Causation); How people reflect on discoveries from the past to inform change (Change); How exploration brings about change and new understandings for people, places and society (Change)

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Explorer Timeline' in Scratch where clicking on explorer sprites along a timeline triggers animations of their voyages, push/pull factor explanations from the First Fleet BTN research, and recorded narration — creating a digital museum where visitors can explore how the factors that motivate exploration shaped society.

Students produce a 'Mars Colony Pitch' video presenting their group's UN Mars colonisation project, explaining how they incorporated at least 3 UN Sustainable Development Goals into their colony design, researched Mars conditions using Epic and Kiddle, and connected exploration of the past to designing for the future.

Students code a Sphero to navigate a floor map tracing the flight path from their researched country to Melbourne, calculating distance and direction — connecting geography, coding and spatial reasoning in a hands-on culminating task.

Students build a Minecraft Education reconstruction of a key location from the exploration era they researched — a First Fleet ship, a colonial settlement, or a Mars colony designed around the UN SDGs from their project. The build must include functional zones (living quarters, food storage, navigation, work areas) and each zone must be accompanied by a written sign explaining its purpose based on the research conducted. For the Mars colony option, students apply their sustainability research directly: every structure must solve a specific survival problem (radiation shielding, water recycling, food growing). The process of building forces students to think through exploration not as a sequence of events but as a set of physical and logistical problems that real explorers had to solve.

Students use Google Earth to trace the routes of the explorers studied during the unit — navigating Burke and Wills' inland journey, the route of the First Fleet, and early Pacific voyages. At each significant location they compare what the satellite imagery shows today with what the explorers would have encountered, discussing what motivated them and what consequences their arrival had for the people and environments already there. The real-world geography gives weight to the unit's inquiry into how exploration leads to discoveries that change society. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

Students use this world collection to travel through history and experience how exploration led to the growth of settlements, trade routes and new communities. They connect what they discover to their Mars Colony project — identifying which historical exploration patterns (resource-seeking, route-finding, settlement-building) apply to future planetary exploration. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/social-studies/World_Cities_and_Civilizations

ci: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
