ci: Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.
lo: How technology expands creative possibilities; The evolution of communication tools; The impact of technology on expression

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Communication Timeline' in Scratch where visitors click on era sprites (ancient, industrial, digital) to see animated demonstrations of how communication tools evolved — each era reveals how technology transformed creative expression, connecting to the KWL chart journey and family interviews about technology changes.

Students create a digital portfolio website showcasing how technology has transformed creative expression, featuring their KWL chart journey, family interview findings about how technology/communication has changed, and examples of their own technology-enhanced creations from the unit.

Students produce a 'Then and Now' documentary comparing historical communication tools with modern technology, incorporating family interview responses about technological change and their personal 'top wonder' about technology's future impact on creative expression.

Students use green screen to film themselves presenting from inside a historical setting, demonstrating the contrast between old and new communication tools explored in their 'Then and Now' documentary, showing firsthand how technology has transformed creative expression.

Students build and code a Lego Spike automated message delivery system, demonstrating how technology has transformed communication from manual to automated processes, connecting their engineering prototype to the evolution of communication tools mapped in their timeline infographic.

ci: Systems connect us.
lo: Human systems; The interconnections that exist within and across systems; The influence of systems on communities

s.d: Students design a 3D model in Tinkercad of an innovative system component that solves a real school community problem (automated lunch ordering, smart timetable display, resource sharing station), with labelled parts showing how interconnections within the system enable it to function — presenting their design alongside their Scratch simulation.

Students build a Lego Spike automated sorting or delivery system demonstrating how interconnected components work together, programming sensors and motors to show what happens when one part of the system fails, connecting to their investigation of how human systems function and PE body systems research.

Students produce a short documentary exploring how a human system functions, connecting to PE investigations of team-based sports (basketball, netball, European handball) where they experienced how systems work through roles, rules, and interconnections.

Students use Mentimeter to map the human systems that connect their school community — posting on a shared board all the systems they can identify (timetabling, food service, transport, communication, wellbeing). The class then votes on which systems they depend on most, and which would cause the greatest disruption if they broke down. The real-time collaborative mapping reveals that systems are invisible until they fail, directly supporting the unit's central idea that interconnected systems connect communities and that understanding them matters.

Students use Minecraft Education to design and build a model community system — a town where transport, food supply, communication, healthcare, and governance zones are all physically connected and interdependent. After building the functioning system, students must deliberately break one connection (remove the road between the farm and the market, block the bridge to the hospital) and document the chain of consequences. The physical visibility of how one broken link cascades through the whole system makes the unit's central idea tangible: interconnections within and across systems are what allow communities to function, and their fragility is only visible when something fails. The build is presented to another group who must identify the broken connection and explain how to restore it.

ci: Changes to the Earth impact environment and communities.
lo: The causes of geological change over time; How extreme environments cause natural disasters; The impact of natural events on communities

s.d: Students create an explanation video about geological changes and natural disasters, using the explanation text type practised during English (Seesaw Explanation Text activities) and incorporating research from digital and print text comparisons about environmental impacts on communities.

Students design a 3D model in Tinkercad of an earthquake-resistant structure — applying the engineering principles they researched about base isolation, shear walls and cross-bracing. They test their design logic against the Lego Spike seismic simulation, comparing how their Tinkercad specifications translate (or fail to translate) into real structural behaviour. The iterative design-test-redesign loop connects the unit's central idea about design processes directly to the specific geological forces they've been studying.

Students build an interactive 3D 'Natural Disaster Museum' in CoSpaces — creating immersive rooms for each disaster type studied (earthquake, tsunami, volcanic eruption, flood) with clickable exhibits showing causes, environmental impacts and community responses. Visitors can walk through each room and access the student-curated evidence about how geological and weather events reshape environments and communities. The museum format gives the unit's research a genuine public audience, and the spatial design challenge deepens students' understanding of how different disaster types operate.

Students build a Lego Spike earthquake-resistant structure incorporating sensors and motors, testing whether their design withstands shaking to connect engineering solutions to their Tinkercad emergency shelter prototype and natural disaster research.

Students program micro:bit accelerometers to simulate seismograph readings during their natural disaster research, shaking the sensor at different intensities to model earthquake measurements, connecting the data to their explanation texts about geological changes and community impacts.

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: The diversity of First Nations people; The importance of community, family and kinship; The importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: Students produce a respectful audio documentary exploring the importance of Country, language and community to First Nations people, incorporating stories shared through class provocations, research from Padlet reflections, and connections between First Nations perspectives and the concept of sharing the planet.

Students code an interactive map of Australia in Scratch where clicking on different regions reveals the language group, key Country features, and cultural practices of the First Nations peoples of that area. Building the map requires genuine research — students must locate accurate information about specific groups, not generic 'Aboriginal culture' content — and the code forces them to represent that knowledge in a structured, navigable form. The interactive map demonstrates respect through specificity: each nation's Country is distinct, named, and accurately represented.

Students build a 3D virtual 'Songlines Experience' in CoSpaces representing the First Nations Country they researched — creating walkable environments where clickable objects reveal the significance of land features, the meaning of place names in the relevant language group, and the custodial responsibilities that connect people to Country. The interactive exhibition is shared with another class, giving the unit's respectful inquiry into diversity of First Nations peoples a genuine audience and emphasising that knowledge of Country is not static history but living culture.

DLP captures aerial footage of the local Elsternwick area and surrounding green spaces, providing a bird's-eye perspective of the land that students compare with First Nations understandings of Country, language groups, and connections to place explored throughout the unit.

Students respond to a series of true/false provocations about the importance of country to first nations people using Mentimeter live polls — the class sees results in real time, debates surprising answers, and identifies misconceptions to investigate during the unit.

Students explore this lesson collection — built in authentic partnership with First Nations communities — to experience the stories, cultural practices, and deep connections to Country of Indigenous peoples from around the world. They identify parallels with Woi wurrung and Boon Wurrung perspectives explored in class, comparing how different First Nations peoples express custodial responsibility through language, land management and community. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/social-studies/Indigenous_Cultures_and_History

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human migration is a response to challenges and opportunities
lo: The reasons people migrate; Migration throughout history; Effects of migration on communities, cultures and individuals

s.d: Planner content not yet uploaded — specific suggestions pending. Please send the UOI planner for this unit to Nathan Benn on Teams to generate tailored technology integration suggestions.

ci: Leaders shape communities.
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; The purpose of leadership in communities; Qualities of influential leaders

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Leadership in Action' simulation in Scratch where players face community challenges and must choose different leadership approaches — each choice triggers animated consequences showing how leadership qualities like communication, empathy and decision-making shape community outcomes, connecting to the Learner Profile exploration.

Students build an interactive 3D 'Leadership Hall of Fame' in CoSpaces where visitors walk through exhibit rooms for each leader studied during library sessions, clicking on portrait objects to trigger research findings about their leadership qualities, impact, and how form (recognisable structure) enhances understanding — creating a virtual museum they can share.

Students produce a podcast profile of their chosen leader — structured not as a biography but as a critical analysis. The episode must answer three questions: What specific leadership quality does this leader exemplify most strongly and why? What is the single biggest criticism of their leadership approach? And what would a different leader have done differently in the same circumstances? The critical-question structure prevents the episode from becoming a fan piece and develops the analytical thinking at the heart of the unit. When the class listens to each other's profiles, patterns emerge across different leaders, different eras, and different communities — building the shared framework for what makes leadership effective that the unit works toward. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students use Freeform collaboratively to build a class leadership web — placing their chosen leader at the centre and mapping the qualities, responsibilities and community impacts that radiate outward. When multiple students map different leaders on the same Freeform canvas, patterns emerge: which leadership qualities appear across all their examples, and which are unique to specific contexts. The shared mapping becomes a class-generated framework for understanding what leadership means across different communities and time periods.

Students post their leader research findings including key facts, leadership qualities, and impact evidence, creating a collaborative class gallery that shows the diversity of leadership explored through library research sessions and Seesaw documentation.

ci: People can create or manipulate messages to target specific audiences.
lo: The devices used to target audiences; Critical evaluation of messages; How we know we have been influenced

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Ad Deconstructor' in Scratch where users drag persuasive devices (colour manipulation, emotional music, celebrity endorsement, urgency language) onto an advertisement template and watch in real time how each device changes the message's impact — demonstrating firsthand how media creators manipulate audiences, connecting to their GarageBand jingle composition and green screen ad production.

Students create a persuasive advertisement targeting a specific audience, demonstrating the media manipulation techniques explored during the unit, and then create a companion 'behind-the-scenes' analysis video explaining the persuasive devices used — connecting to the Lume Museum visit exploring Van Gogh's visual storytelling techniques.

Students produce a short film demonstrating how messages can be manipulated to influence audiences, featuring before/after versions of the same content presented differently — connected to the unit's focus on understanding media representations and modes of presentation.

Students produce a media literacy podcast episode critically evaluating a message aimed at their age group, applying the persuasive analysis skills practised through class activities and connecting to the ethical use of media/information focus. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students use green screen to produce their persuasive advertisement, demonstrating firsthand how media creators manipulate settings, backgrounds, and visual context to target specific audiences, connecting the production experience to their critical analysis of media manipulation techniques.

ci: Entrepreneurs have a responsibility to develop, market and sell sustainable products and services.
lo: The power of giving back; How our environment is impacted by the products we create; Sustainable business practices

s.d: Students build a website for their sustainable product business, including product information, sustainability credentials, marketing materials, and data collected about community needs — connecting to the market stall activity where they developed, marketed and sold products.

Students produce a professional advertisement for their sustainable product, applying marketing and persuasive techniques while demonstrating responsible entrepreneurship — connecting to Dance & Drama exploration of myth's role in various cultures and storytelling for marketing.

Students build a Lego Spike prototype or automated production line for their sustainable product business, programming motors and sensors to demonstrate how their product could be manufactured responsibly, connecting engineering to their market research data tracked in Microsoft Excel.

Students design a business pitch presentation for their sustainable product, incorporating market research data (collected and interpreted during Maths connections), sustainability analysis of environmental impact, and a 'giving back' component showing their product's community benefit.

Students use Mentimeter to pitch their sustainable product to the class in a live investor panel format — presenting their key sustainability claim, then facing real-time audience voting on whether they'd invest, whether the environmental claim is credible, and what the product's biggest weakness is. The instant visual feedback from the Mentimeter results creates a genuine accountability moment: students must respond to the data in real time, adjusting their pitch or defending their position, connecting responsible entrepreneurship to honest market feedback.

ci: Design processes enable people to respond creatively to challenges in the natural world.
lo: The nature and impact of natural hazards (Causation); How design processes are used to generate and test solutions (Function); The ethical and practical considerations in human responses to natural challenges (Responsibility)

s.d: Students build an interactive 3D disaster response scenario in CoSpaces showing their Operation Earth Action design solution in situ — visitors walk through the Lismore flood environment and interact with the earthquake-resistant structure, experiencing the ethical and practical considerations from multiple stakeholder perspectives explored during the empathic design interviews.

Students design a 3D prototype of an earthquake-resistant structure meeting the design constraints (36cm tall, must hold specific weight), applying the full design thinking cycle introduced through the UNICEF Workshop and iterating based on shake-table testing during Prototyping Week.

Students produce a persuasive pitch presentation explaining their Operation Earth Action design solution, using the persuasive pitch structure to communicate their process, purpose, and design decisions — connected to the school showcase and pitch presentations culminating activity.

Students build a Lego Spike seismic alert system with sensors that detect vibrations and trigger a motor-driven warning mechanism, connecting engineering design to their earthquake research and the Lismore flood case study about community response to natural hazards.

Students use micro:bit accelerometers and sensors to test their earthquake-resistant structure prototypes during the Week 8 prototyping phase, collecting data on structural movement and stability to evaluate their designs against criteria like usability, empathy, and effectiveness.

ci: Finding peaceful solutions to conflict can make a difference in the world.
lo: Types of conflict; Causes and impacts of conflict; Conflict resolution

s.d: Students design a 3D model in Tinkercad of a 'Peace Hub' — a community space designed to facilitate peaceful conflict resolution, with specific architectural features that reflect the conflict resolution principles they've studied (open sight lines for transparency, separate entry points for de-escalation, shared gathering spaces for dialogue). The design challenge requires students to translate abstract peacebuilding principles into physical form, arguing how their spatial decisions support the unit's inquiry into what makes conflict resolution actually work.

Students produce a podcast episode investigating a real current conflict — researching its causes, the parties involved, how it has escalated, and what peaceful resolution attempts have been made or proposed. The episode must include at least two perspectives (ideally from primary or secondary sources on different sides) and close with a reasoned proposal for a peaceful pathway forward. The podcast format requires students to hold complexity without collapsing it: they must represent multiple perspectives fairly before arguing for a resolution, which is precisely the skill peacekeeping and mediation require. Episodes are shared across the class as a collective study in how conflict resolution works — and doesn't — in the real world. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students code an interactive 'Choose Your Own Adventure' conflict resolution simulation, where users navigate a conflict scenario making decisions that lead to peaceful or escalated outcomes — connecting to the concepts of perspective and connection explored through Drama improvisation.

Students investigate how drone technology is used in humanitarian aid and conflict zones (delivering medical supplies, mapping disaster areas), programming CoDrone EDU flight paths to simulate aid delivery routes and debating the ethical considerations, connecting to their research on peaceful solutions to real-world conflicts.

Students use Freeform to build a collaborative conflict analysis map connecting types of conflict to their causes, impacts and potential peaceful resolutions explored during the unit — plotting how humanitarian drone technology, peacekeeping strategies and community action interconnect.

Students explore the 'Peace Builders' world — developed with the Nobel Peace Center and Games for Change — following Nobel Peace Prize Laureates navigating real conflict situations. They analyse the strategies each laureate used to find peaceful resolution, comparing their approaches with the conflict resolution frameworks studied in class. The world makes the unit's central idea — that peaceful solutions can make a difference — tangible through the documented decisions of real peacemakers. Access at education.minecraft.net/lessons/peace-builders

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has shaped society.
lo: The impact of colonisation; Colonisation stories; The effects of colonisation — past, present and future

s.d: Students create a research website examining the impact of colonisation on a specific region, incorporating perspectives from the 'Unsettled' resource (exploring 'How do we read the signs around us?'), Chinese language geography vocabulary, and analysis of how colonisation's effects continue into the present and future.

Students code an interactive 'Colonisation: Past, Present, Future' timeline in Scratch where clicking on era sprites triggers animated sequences showing the impact on specific communities — incorporating primary and secondary sources from their research and perspectives from the 'Unsettled' exhibition and carved spotted gum investigation.

Students film first-person historical accounts using green screen with period-appropriate backgrounds, performing as characters from colonisation eras to bring primary sources to life, incorporating the footage into their iMovie documentary alongside research from the carved canoe investigation and Colonisation: Past, Present, Future book.

Students produce a podcast episode that presents colonisation through two voices — one episode, two perspectives on the same event. One student records as a coloniser explaining their motivations and actions; a second records as an Indigenous Australian responding to the consequences of those exact same actions and decisions. The scripts are researched, not invented: students draw on primary sources, the 'Unsettled' resource, and the carved spotted gum investigation to ground each voice in historical evidence. Playing both tracks to the class demonstrates the unit's central idea with precision: colonisation didn't just transform society — it transformed it differently depending entirely on whose story is being told and whose land, language, and community bore the cost. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students use Google Earth to map the geographic spread of colonisation studied during the unit — tracing the routes of European colonisers from their home countries to the territories they claimed, dropping pins at key sites of first contact, resistance, and lasting impact. Navigating the true distances and geographic barriers involved reveals the scale of what colonisation meant physically, and the satellite perspective on present-day land use connects historical dispossession to contemporary reality in ways that text descriptions cannot. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

ci: People's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values and can impact others.
lo: How values and beliefs impact choices; How people's choices and actions impact others; Responsible decision-making

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Values Dilemma Simulator' in Scratch where players face ethical scenarios and must choose actions based on different belief systems — each choice triggers animated consequences showing how values and beliefs influence decisions and impact others, connecting to PE team outcome reflections and the Freeform concept map exploration.

Students produce a short documentary exploring how values and beliefs influence decision-making, featuring personal reflections from PE lessons where they experienced how responsible choices in team activities affected others, and connecting to music lessons about how personal actions impact group performance.

Students build an interactive 3D 'Perspectives Gallery' in CoSpaces where visitors walk through rooms representing different scenarios where values and beliefs led to different decisions, clicking on character objects to hear recorded analyses of causes, impacts and responsible decision-making approaches — creating a virtual ethical reasoning exhibition.

Students use Mentimeter word clouds and live polling to gather anonymous class responses about values dilemmas and belief scenarios, building real-time data on how perspectives differ across the group and using the visualisations as evidence in their analytical posters mapping beliefs to choices and impacts.

Students produce a podcast episode exploring a real ethical dilemma from the unit — a situation where personal beliefs and values led to a decision that impacted others. They research different perspectives on the dilemma, interview a classmate who holds a different view, and present their own position with reasoning. The podcast format requires students to hold an opinion under scrutiny and explain it clearly, building the communication and ethical reasoning skills at the heart of the unit's central idea that our choices and values can make a positive or harmful difference in the world. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

ci: An inquiry into the ways in which we discover and express ideas, feelings, nature, culture, beliefs and values.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

ci: Student-led exhibition (PYPEX) – Students develop and present their own units of inquiry.
lo: Student-led transdisciplinary themes; Approaches to learning; Research and presentation skills

s.d: Students code an interactive PYPEX exhibition guide in Scratch where visitors navigate through their unit of inquiry — clicking on topic sprites to access research findings, transdisciplinary connections, and media elements — creating a digital companion experience to their physical exhibition that demonstrates research and presentation skills at their most ambitious.

Students build a professional exhibition website for their PYPEX unit of inquiry, featuring their research findings, transdisciplinary connections displayed using the unit outline template, and a Venn diagram comparing their research topic in Australia and China (or their home country).

Students produce a documentary showcasing their PYPEX inquiry journey from the Launch Day provocation through to their final presentation, demonstrating research and referencing skills, file management, and their ability to present findings to an audience of parents and community members.

Students create a podcast series documenting their PYPEX research process, including interviews, reflections on their Essential Agreement creation, and discussion of their shared issue — building ICT media representation and critical evaluation skills. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students use green screen technology to film segments of their PYPEX documentary, placing themselves within contexts relevant to their unit of inquiry topic, combining the footage with their research presentations and interview recordings in the final exhibition showcase.

ci: Energy may be converted, transformed, and used to support human progress.
lo: Different forms of energy and how they are used; The impact of different energy sources on the planet; Sustainable energy practices

s.d: Students design a 3D model in Tinkercad of their Solar Buddy light incorporating a custom housing that optimises solar energy capture, with labelled components showing how energy converts from solar to electrical to light — presenting their digital design alongside their working Solar Buddy device and micro:bit voltage data to demonstrate energy transformation in both virtual and physical form.

Students produce a science documentary explaining energy conversion and transformation, incorporating footage of their Solar Buddy light construction, electrical circuit experiments, and the Rube Goldberg machine chain reaction analysis — using the science experiment report writing skills developed during English.

Students build and code a renewable energy model (wind turbine with motor or solar tracker) that demonstrates energy conversion from one form to another, collecting data on output and connecting their findings to the unit's investigation of sustainable energy practices and individual responsibilities for promoting them.

Students explore how drones convert battery energy into kinetic energy for flight, programming CoDrone EDU to perform controlled manoeuvres while discussing energy transformation chains (chemical → electrical → kinetic), connecting the hands-on experience to their broader research on how energy supports human progress.

Students create an 'Energy Transformation' investigation journal documenting their experiments with electrical circuits, dynamos (connecting William Kamkwamba's bicycle headlight dynamo story to modern generators), and Solar Buddy lights — including procedural writing, labelled diagrams, and evidence-based conclusions about sustainable energy practices.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for sustainable practices; Taking action for environmental stewardship

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Environmental Stewardship Dashboard' in Scratch where visitors click on community action sprites to see animated data visualisations of environmental impact — tracking metrics from their action project (green space percentages, water usage, biodiversity counts) with clickable graphs and progress indicators that demonstrate how community values translate into measurable environmental change.

DLP captures 360° footage of local Elsternwick environmental sites — the foreshore, local parks, community gardens and development boundaries — creating immersive reference material students revisit in class. Using the 360° footage as their evidence base, students identify specific examples of how community values shape environmental management: where has the community chosen to protect green space? Where has development won? The immersive footage gives students the observational richness of being on-site without leaving the classroom, and forms the visual backbone of their action project websites.

Students create an environmental stewardship project website presenting their action project (developed during the 'Finalize and share action projects' culminating activity), featuring their KWL chart journey from provocation images through to their final proposals for promoting environmental responsibility.

Students use Minecraft Education to model the local Elsternwick environmental landscape — mapping green corridors, foreshore areas, development zones, and community gardens using the Insta360 footage and local environmental data gathered during the unit as their spatial reference. They then simulate two contrasting futures side by side: one where current development patterns continue (green space fragments, wildlife corridors break) and one where the community stewardship values they've researched are implemented (corridors reconnected, native planting expanded, development buffered). The two-world comparison externalises the unit's central inquiry: community values don't just shape how we feel about the environment — they physically determine what it looks like in ten years.

Students prototype an animal movement tracker using the micro:bit's accelerometer and radio features — programming one micro:bit to detect and transmit movement patterns and a second to receive and display them. They simulate conservation scientists tracking how wildlife is affected by habitat loss, then reflect on how this data could inform local environmental management decisions. The project connects directly to the unit's taking-action focus: students propose how real tracking data could be used by their community to protect local species and drive the environmental stewardship they've researched throughout the unit.

Students explore the Sustainability City world — where each district models a real sustainable system: solar energy, waste recycling, responsible forestry, water management. They analyse each system, identify how it reduces environmental impact, and redesign one district to model a stewardship intervention for their local area. The build challenges students to apply the unit's central idea — that community actions affect the balance of nature — as a design and systems-thinking problem. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/science-subject-kit/sustainability

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Significant events shape a place
lo: What is a significant event; Why these events occurred; Impact of significant events

s.d: Planner content not yet uploaded — specific suggestions pending. Please send the UOI planner for this unit to Nathan Benn on Teams to generate tailored technology integration suggestions.

ci: Personal changes and influences contribute to our evolving sense of self.
lo: Physical, social, emotional and intellectual changes that occur throughout life; Influences that shape our identity; Developing a sense of self

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Personal Identity Timeline' in Scratch where visitors click through a character's life stages — childhood, adolescence, adulthood — seeing how physical, social, emotional and intellectual changes interact with cultural influences, relationships and personal choices to shape identity over time. Building the simulation requires students to model the unit's central idea — that personal changes and influences contribute to an evolving sense of self — as a system with real cause-and-effect logic, deepening understanding by encoding it into code.

Students produce a podcast episode exploring the single biggest influence on their sense of self — family, culture, peer relationships, digital identity, or a significant personal experience — and how that influence has shaped who they are becoming. The episode is personal but analytical: students must explain the influence, give specific examples of how it shaped their values or behaviours, and reflect on whether that shaping has been positive, complicated, or both. The audio format gives students a degree of privacy that video doesn't — they can speak candidly about identity without performing for a camera — and the act of articulating their evolving sense of self in their own voice is itself the unit's summative task. A curated selection of episodes (with student permission) creates a Year 6 legacy archive. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students create a reflective identity journal documenting their personal growth journey through Year 6, featuring their owl self-portrait design, genre exploration from Literature/Library provocation about selecting texts that are a 'good fit', and analysis of how changes and influences contributed to their evolving sense of self.

Students use Freeform to create a personal 'Identity Timeline' board — mapping the physical, emotional, social and intellectual changes they've experienced or anticipate, the influences that have shaped their sense of self (family, culture, media, peers), and their current values and aspirations. The board externalises the unit's abstract inquiry into personal identity, making it discussable and revisable as new learning challenges earlier assumptions throughout the unit.

Students use Mentimeter live polling and word clouds to anonymously share perspectives on identity influences, personal changes, and growth milestones, generating class-wide data visualisations that feed into their 'Sense of Self' analytical posters and personal documentary reflections.

ci: Emotions influence our wellbeing.
lo: How our feelings and emotions affect us; How feelings and emotions are interpreted; How our emotions and behaviour affect relationships

s.d: Students code a 'Colour Monster' interactive story in Scratch Jr where the Colour Monster character changes colour based on different scenarios — moving to a new school (yellow/worried), making a friend (pink/happy), losing a toy (blue/sad). Each scene includes a voice recording of the student explaining the management strategy for that emotion, connecting to the Zones of Regulation framework explored throughout the unit.

Students compose a 'Feelings Soundscape Suite' — four short GarageBand compositions, each representing one Zone of Regulation (blue=low energy, green=ready to learn, yellow=heightened, red=extremely heightened). They select instruments, tempo and volume to match each zone, then present the suite to the class who must guess which zone each piece represents — proving they understand how emotions can be expressed through music, not just words.

Students create a personal 'Emotions Dictionary' in Book Creator — one page per Colour Monster zone (yellow joy, blue sadness, red anger, black fear, green calm) with their own illustrations, a written description of what that feeling feels like in their body, and a voice recording describing a time they felt it. The multimodal dictionary (drawing + writing + voice) makes abstract emotional states concrete and revisable — a resource students return to throughout the unit and the year as they encounter new situations that activate each zone.

Students program Beebots to travel to emotion stations around the classroom — at each stop, the child names the emotion on the card and acts it out, connecting physical movement and coding sequences to the emotional vocabulary explored through the Colour Monster story.

Students use the Seesaw drawing tool to illustrate the Colour Monster zone they're feeling right now — choosing colours, drawing their own monster face and recording a voice note explaining the emotion, what caused it, and one strategy for managing it. The multimodal entry (drawing + voice) makes abstract emotional states visible and gives the teacher a real-time snapshot of the class's wellbeing before deepening the inquiry into how our emotions affect ourselves and others.

ci: The products people use go through a process of change.
lo: Where food comes from; How food can be changed for different purposes; An appreciation of food

s.d: Students produce a 'From Farm to Fork' mini documentary — each group films one food's journey using toy props and real food items as actors (a toy cow becomes milk, flour becomes bread, an egg becomes a cake). They use stop-motion for the transformation scenes and narrate the process using sequencing language (first, then, next, finally), creating a visual story of how products change that couldn't exist without video editing. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students code a 'Food Journey' animation in Scratch Jr showing the step-by-step process of one food product changing from raw material to finished product — a wheat seed grows into a plant, gets harvested, ground into flour, mixed into dough, baked into bread, and eaten. Each stage is a new scene with characters that move and transform, building a coded model of the process of change that plays automatically.

Students film themselves as 'food reporters' in front of the green screen, transporting themselves to the different stages of the food journey — standing in a wheat field, inside a flour mill, in a bakery kitchen, at a supermarket. They narrate each stage of the process, explaining how the food changed and why, creating a news-style report that takes the audience on a journey through places they couldn't physically visit in one lesson. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

DLP captures 360° footage at the Chesterfield Farm excursion, students explore the immersive video back in class to revisit the farm-to-food journey and describe what they observed about where food comes from.

Students explore Chesterfield Farm on Google Earth satellite view before the excursion, spotting the paddocks, barns, and animal enclosures from above. They predict what food comes from which area of the farm, then compare their predictions with what they actually discover during the visit — building geographic awareness and connecting the 'bird's-eye view' to the real experience of where food comes from. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

ci: The properties of materials influence how objects move and their use.
lo: Properties of materials; How objects move; How we use materials

s.d: Students film their materials testing investigation and narrate their findings in a Canva Video — holding up each material, describing its properties (hard/soft, rough/smooth, heavy/light), and explaining what they discovered about how it affects movement. The finished video is their science report: a record of real observations presented with a title, simple transitions and their own voice as the scientist — connecting the physical testing of materials directly to the concept that properties determine use.

Students produce a 'Materials Detectives' investigation video — filming themselves conducting the 'Design a Floater' challenge, capturing the moment their creation sinks or floats in slow motion, then explaining to camera which material properties made the difference. Each group tests a different material combination, and the videos are compiled into a class investigation reel showing the full range of results. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students create an interactive 'Materials Museum' book where each page features a different material explored during the unit — with a photo of the material, an embedded voice recording of the student describing its properties using scientific vocabulary (rough, smooth, hard, soft, transparent, opaque), and a video clip of the material being tested on the ramp or in the float/sink investigation. The museum book is shared with parents as a digital exhibition.

Students place colour cards along a path representing different material properties (hard, soft, rough, smooth), programming Indi Robot to follow the trail and stop at each station where they describe the material, connecting colour-coded sorting to the material classification activities from the mystery bag provocation.

Students program Beebots to move across different material surfaces (carpet, tile, cardboard) placed along the path, predicting and observing how the material properties affect movement — connecting to the slide-and-roll investigation findings.

ci: An inquiry into rights and responsibilities in the struggle to share finite resources with other people and with other living things.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Planner content not yet uploaded — specific suggestions pending. Please send the UOI planner for this unit to Nathan Benn on Teams to generate tailored technology integration suggestions.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Family histories shape who we are and what we value
lo: What makes a family; The people in your family; Your family history; What is important to your family

s.d: Students produce a 'My Family' mini documentary — filming their family tree artwork (created with autumn leaf rubbings), the special family item they brought from home, and a to-camera interview where they explain where their family comes from and what traditions matter most. The teacher adds family photos (sent by parents) as cutaway images, creating a personal family history film that becomes a treasured keepsake — shared at Student Led Conference.

Students film themselves in front of the green screen 'visiting' a place connected to their family history — their grandparents' country, the hospital where they were born, the first home their family lived in. They narrate the story of why this place matters to their family while the green screen transports them there, creating a personal family history documentary that brings distant places into the classroom. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code a 'My Family Timeline' animation in Scratch Jr showing key moments — grandparents' story, parents meeting, the student being born, starting school. Characters appear, backgrounds change between scenes, and the student records voice narration explaining each moment. The timeline plays automatically, creating a coded family story that the student built themselves — shared at Student Led Conference as proof of their learning.

Students record a short 'family story' podcast in GarageBand — retelling a favourite family memory shared by a parent or grandparent, adding background music that matches the mood of the story. The recordings are played during circle time, creating an oral history archive that connects to the unit's focus on how family histories shape who we are — audio storytelling that couldn't exist without recording and layering tools.

Students use Google Maps Street View to explore the streets around Glen Waverley — finding the school entrance, the local park, the shops on Kingsway — and take a screenshot of their favourite discovery. In Seesaw they annotate the screenshot with the drawing tool, marking places that matter to their family and recording a voice note explaining their family's connection to those locations. The map becomes a personalised artefact connecting the unit's inquiry into family histories and what families value to the actual place where students live.

ci: Schools are organised to help students learn and work together
lo: The organisation of the Junior School; Routines that allow developing independence; How to make the classroom and playground healthy and safe

s.d: Students produce a 'Welcome to Our School' video tour — each small group films a section of the school (library, classroom, playground, office) with a student guide explaining the routines and rules that make that space work. The teacher edits the segments into one continuous tour with title cards and transitions, creating a real resource that could be shown to new Prep families at orientation — their work has genuine purpose beyond the classroom.

Students code a 'My School Day' animation in Scratch Jr showing the daily routine sequence — a character arrives at school, unpacks their bag, sits on the mat for morning circle, goes to the library, eats lunch, plays outside, packs up. Each scene demonstrates a routine that builds independence, and the animation plays automatically when the green flag is pressed — the student built a working model of their school day.

Students dress up as different school helpers (teacher, librarian, crossing guard, principal) and film themselves in front of the green screen explaining their role — with the green screen placing them in the actual school location where that person works. The class watches all the performances together, connecting each role to the routines that help the school community function. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students use colour-coded cards to program Indi Robot to follow the sequence of their daily school routines (pack bag → line up → enter classroom → morning circle), connecting the step-by-step coding to the independence skills and routines practised throughout the unit.

Students program Cubetto to navigate a floor map of the campus — visiting the library, classroom, office, and oval in a sequence they design themselves. Before programming, they plan their route with the physical coding blocks, predicting how many forward moves and turns they need. When Cubetto doesn't arrive where expected, students debug by identifying exactly which instruction went wrong. The process of planning, executing and correcting a physical journey maps directly to the unit's inquiry into how the school is organised and how systems and routines help people work together.

ci: People use imagination and materials to express stories, ideas and culture through performance.
lo: Different materials to make puppets; Types and uses of puppets by different cultures; Features and purposes of puppet performances

s.d: Students code a digital puppet show in Scratch Jr — programming puppet characters to walk on stage, speak dialogue, interact with each other and take a bow. Unlike the physical puppet performance the class creates with handmade puppets, this version runs autonomously — the student wrote both the script AND the stage directions in code. Performing the same story through two completely different mediums reveals how the choice of form shapes meaning, connecting directly to the unit's exploration of how different cultures use puppets to tell stories and ideas.

Students film their puppet performance in front of the green screen, placing their handmade puppets (shadow puppets, popsicle stick puppets from French AIM Play, hand puppets) against culturally appropriate backdrops — a Balinese shadow puppet against a temple, a French puppet in a Parisian street, a sock puppet in a living room. The green screen transforms simple classroom puppets into world-travelling performers. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students film their puppet performance using the puppets they constructed from various materials, adding titles and simple transitions to present their cultural story — building on vocabulary about features and purpose developed through watching puppet performances.

Students create a 30-second 'puppet soundtrack' in GarageBand — choosing instruments and sounds that match the personality of a puppet character they imagine (drums for a brave puppet, gentle chimes for a shy puppet, fast piano for a funny puppet). They play their soundtrack to a partner who draws what they think the puppet looks like — discovering that sound is another way to express character before the class explores puppets from different cultures.

DLP captures 360° footage of the Year 1 puppet performance showcase, preserving the cultural puppet performances from multiple angles so students can revisit and reflect on how each group expressed their story. Students watch the immersive footage and compare how the same story feels different when performed by a shadow puppet, a hand puppet, and a popsicle stick puppet — directly connecting to the unit's inquiry into how different cultures use different materials and performance styles to express ideas and stories.

ci: Communities work together by creating systems to help people live and learn.
lo: The roles people play in a community; Systems and routines that help us stay organised; How communities work together to meet needs

s.d: Students build a Lego Spike community vehicle (postal van, fire truck, ambulance) with a working motor, then program it to follow a route on their class community map — stopping at locations where the service is needed. Each team presents their vehicle explaining what system it belongs to and what happens if it breaks down, connecting the engineering build to their investigation of how interdependent community systems help people.

Students produce a 'Day Without Systems' mockumentary — filming a comedy sketch showing what happens when one community system fails (the library has no borrowing rules so books disappear, the canteen has no queue system so lunch is chaos, the crossing has no guard so nobody can cross). The humour makes the learning memorable while demonstrating the central idea that communities need systems to function.

Students film themselves as different community workers in front of the green screen — placed inside a hospital, fire station, post office, school office. Each student explains their role and demonstrates how their system connects to other community systems ('I'm the librarian — I organise books so teachers can find resources for lessons'), creating an interconnected web of community roles visible in one video compilation. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code a 'Community Systems' animation in Scratch Jr showing what happens when a community worker does their job vs when they're absent — the postal worker delivers mail and people smile, then the scene replays without them and letters pile up, demonstrating how every role connects to the system.

Students explore the streets around Glen Waverley on Google Maps Street View, spotting community systems in action — the traffic lights controlling cars, the library building where books are shared, the fire station where help comes from. They screenshot three systems they find and the class discusses what would happen if each one disappeared — launching the inquiry into how communities work together.

ci: Movement is dependent on different forces.
lo: Ways objects move; The influence different materials have on movement; How designs can be adapted and improved

s.d: Students code a Scratch Jr simulation testing how different forces affect movement — programming a sprite to move slowly across a 'rough' background (representing friction) then fast across a 'smooth' one, or sending it rolling down a ramp backdrop and stopping at different points. They adjust speed and direction values to model push, pull and gravity, comparing their coded predictions with the physical ramp experiments they ran in class. The code makes the invisible force visible — a direct extension of the unit's inquiry into how different forces influence the way objects move.

Students build a Lego Spike catapult launcher that flings a ball across the room, then modify the launch angle, arm length and rubber band tension to change how far and how high the ball travels — recording each test with labelled slow-motion video, graphing their distances, and presenting their 'best design' alongside a poster explaining which force variables made the biggest difference.

Students produce a 'Mythbusters Junior' investigation video where they test a force-related claim from the unit (e.g., 'heavier objects always fall faster', 'smooth surfaces are always slippery'), filming their experiment with multiple camera angles, adding slow-motion replays of key moments, and delivering a verdict with evidence — connected to the ramp building and spinning tops that generated high engagement. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students use micro:bit accelerometers to measure the force of pushes and pulls during their ramp experiments, comparing readings when they change the angle or surface — collecting real data that connects their hands-on investigations to the scientific concepts of contact and non-contact forces.

Students use Google Earth to explore the habitats of invertebrates studied during the unit — navigating to coral reefs to find sea creatures, rainforest canopy for butterfly species, the school garden area from satellite, and polar regions for adaptations. At each location they identify features of the habitat that explain why specific invertebrates can survive there, connecting their structural features research to real geographic environments. The satellite perspective makes the unit's central idea about structure and survival in different environments visible at landscape scale. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

ci: Invertebrates are essential to living things and the environment.
lo: The characteristics of invertebrates; The interdependence of invertebrates and their environment; Life cycles of invertebrates

s.d: Students produce an 'Invertebrate Investigators' documentary — each group films their chosen invertebrate (if found in the school garden) or uses close-up photos and drawings as visual evidence, adding slow-motion footage of insects moving and narrating key facts about structural features, habitat and life cycle. The documentary uses the scientific vocabulary built throughout the unit and includes the 'key learning' that invertebrates have no backbone. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students code a life cycle animation in Scratch Jr showing their chosen invertebrate transforming through each stage — egg to larva to pupa to adult for butterflies, or egg to nymph to adult for grasshoppers. Characters change shape, grow, and move to new backgrounds between scenes, creating a coded model of the life cycle that plays automatically and can be compared with the physical life cycle models built during science.

Students film themselves as 'garden reporters' in front of the green screen, transported inside the school garden ecosystem at macro scale — standing among giant leaves and towering flowers as if they were the size of an invertebrate. They report on the invertebrate they studied, explaining how its structural features help it survive in this environment, connecting the library focus on underwater invertebrates to the school garden investigations. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

DLP captures 360° footage of the school garden ecosystem, students explore the immersive video to spot invertebrates in their natural habitat and document observations about where plants and animals live.

Students photograph each stage of the broad bean growth experiment and use the Seesaw drawing tool to annotate their photos — labelling the structural features they observe (roots, stem, leaves, flowers) and recording a voice note explaining how each feature supports the plant's survival. The annotated photo series becomes a longitudinal science record tracking how structural features develop over the unit, connecting the hands-on observation work directly to the inquiry into how living things' structures allow them to survive in different environments.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: An understanding of history helps us to see how the past shapes the future.
lo: Ways in which children's lives have changed over time; Reasons for changes to children's lives over time

s.d: Students produce a 'Then and Now Museum' video — each group sets up a table display with a toy from the past (from the incursion) next to its modern equivalent, then films a museum-guide-style walkthrough explaining the differences, when the change happened, and why. Slow-motion close-ups highlight the details (clockwork mechanism vs electronic chip, wooden blocks vs plastic Lego), and student guides deliver the verdict: 'the biggest change was...' This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students become 'Time Travellers' using the green screen — transported to a 1950s classroom, a Victorian-era street, or a playground from their grandparents' childhood. Standing inside the historical scene, they narrate what they notice has changed and what has stayed the same, comparing the past environment with their own school experience. The impossible physical journey to another era becomes a lived perspective, making the abstract concept of historical change something students inhabit rather than just read about. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code a 'Toy Timeline' animation in Scratch Jr showing how a toy evolved through history — a wooden spinning top transforms into a wind-up toy, then into a battery-powered version, then into an iPad game. Each stage is a new scene with the toy changing shape and the background shifting from old-fashioned to modern, creating a coded model of change over time that plays automatically.

Students create 'Sounds of Then and Now' — recording pairs of sounds in GarageBand that represent past vs present versions of the same thing (a hand bell vs a phone ringtone, a music box vs a speaker playing Spotify, a typewriter clicking vs a keyboard tapping). The paired sounds become an audio provocation played during circle time, demonstrating change over time through a medium students can hear but not see.

Students find their home on Google Maps, screenshot the Street View, and annotate it in Seesaw — marking the route they walk to school, noting what has changed on their street compared to old photos (from Google Street View's historical imagery), and recording a voice note explaining what the place tells about their family's history. The comparison between Street View time periods gives students a data-driven way to observe how their own local place has changed, connecting personal geography to the unit's central idea that history shapes the present.

ci: Emotions, values, and relationships guide how we connect and collaborate
lo: The function of different parts of the brain; Situations that cause pleasant and unpleasant feelings; Actions that support wellbeing

s.d: Students perform role-plays in front of the green screen showing how their thoughts, feelings and actions determine relationship quality — placed in realistic settings (playground, classroom, home) via the green screen. Each group demonstrates a different scenario: one shows exclusion and repair, another shows cooperation during a challenge, another shows resolving a disagreement — creating a library of relationship strategies the class can rewatch. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students produce a 'Wellbeing Warriors' documentary interviewing classmates about the States of Mind practices that help them most — filming on location at the places around school where they practise mindfulness, perseverance and emotional management. The documentary includes slow-motion footage of PE cooperative activities showing teamwork in action, with student narrators connecting the strategies to real moments. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students create a 'Feelings Playlist' in GarageBand — composing four short pieces, each representing a different emotional state explored through the unit. They select instruments, tempo and dynamics to communicate each feeling, then present the playlist to the class who must identify which emotion each piece represents. The playlist becomes a class resource played during transitions and calm-down time.

Students code a simple two-scene Scratch Jr animation showing a relationship scenario — Scene 1: two characters are in conflict (not sharing, arguing), Scene 2: the characters use a strategy from the Colour Monster vocabulary (talk about feelings, take turns, walk away and breathe). The animation runs automatically, creating a visual model of how actions determine relationship quality.

Students add to their ongoing wellbeing book, documenting new strategies for managing emotions with drawings, photos of PE cooperative activities, and voice recordings explaining how their understanding of relationships has grown through the Boy by Phil Cummings discussions and States of Mind activities.

ci: Storytellers use different methods to convey meaning.
lo: Mediums and techniques artists use; Connections between story and illustrations; How ideas and information are communicated

s.d: Students film a green screen 'Story Museum' presentation where they transport themselves inside the settings of stories explored during the unit, performing as curators who demonstrate how the same story changes when told through different mediums (writing, illustration, drama, digital) — connecting to the author incursion with Sarah Allen about how books are created. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code an animated retelling of their Talk For Writing story in Scratch Jr — programming characters to move through settings with code-driven timing that makes characters appear, speak, and exit on cue. Every pause, entrance, and scene change is a deliberate decision that helps students understand how storytellers control timing and atmosphere. The code IS the storyboard, extending the lessons from the author incursion with Sarah Allen about how every story decision shapes meaning, and demonstrating that the same story told through a different medium creates a completely different experience.

Students produce a short film retelling a story using different media, incorporating illustrations inspired by Indigenous Australian artists (from Visual Art) showing how art is used to tell stories and preserve knowledge.

Students create a soundscape or audio story using music and narration to explore how sound conveys meaning, comparing this medium to the visual storytelling and illustration methods explored through the author visit.

Students contribute to a class 'Storytelling Methods' Padlet — each group posts their Talk For Writing story told through a different medium (written, illustrated, Scratch Jr animated, performed, GarageBand soundscape) so the whole class can compare how the same story feels different across formats. Students comment on each other's entries noting which medium they found most powerful and why, generating the class's own evidence for the unit's central idea that storytellers use different methods to convey meaning.

ci: A sequence of processes enables a system to function.
lo: The systems in our lives; The purpose of various systems; How systems are made of many interdependent parts

s.d: Students produce an iMovie 'Systems Breakdown' documentary where they deliberately remove one step from a familiar system (the café lunch sequence, a marble run, their morning routine) and film what happens — using time-lapse and split-screen to show the working system vs the broken one, proving how a sequence of processes enables a system to function. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students build a Lego Spike automated production line where the motor, sensor and sequence of coded steps must work together to move an object from start to finish — if any part fails, the system stops, demonstrating how systems are made of many interdependent parts and connecting to the 'morning of chaos' provocation where classroom systems were removed.

Students code an interactive animation showing how a system works step-by-step (e.g. cow to milk, wheat to bread — the food production systems explored during the unit), demonstrating how a sequence of processes enables a system to function.

Students program Cubetto to follow the sequence of steps in the café lunch system (kitchen → preparation → serving → student), debugging when steps are out of order to understand how systems break down — connecting to the mini incursion to the school café exploring Gayle's daily systems.

Students use Seesaw to document the class's 'lunch system' investigation — photographing each step of the canteen process they observed, then recording a voice-over explanation of what happens at each stage and why that step is essential for the system to function. The sequenced photo essay, shared with the class, demonstrates that every step in the system has a purpose and removing any one step breaks the whole — connecting directly to the unit's central idea that a sequence of processes enables a system to function.

ci: Changes to materials affect how we use and reuse them in daily life.
lo: Understanding material properties; Reuse and recycling through physical change; Sustainably managing materials

s.d: Students produce an iMovie 'Mythbusters Junior: Materials Edition' episode where they test a claim about material properties (e.g. 'all paper tears the same way'), filming their bending, stretching, breaking and crumpling experiments with slow-motion replay and multiple camera angles, then deliver a verdict based on evidence from their sets of materials changed in different ways. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students build a Lego Spike automated material sorting machine that uses sensors to classify objects by properties (hard/soft, flexible/rigid), programming the motor to push items into labelled bins — connecting their 'odd one out' classification reasoning to an engineering solution and presenting how the machine's sorting rules mirror First Nations Australians' (WESMOB) deep knowledge of natural material properties.

Students build a micro:bit 'Material Change Detector' that uses the temperature sensor to monitor heating experiments in real time, displaying rising/falling temperature readings on the LED screen and logging whether each change is reversible or irreversible — presenting their data at the art exhibit alongside sustainable reuse creations.

Students photograph and classify their material change experiments (crumpled, folded, torn paper; stretched, bent, heated materials), creating labelled before/after comparisons and recording voice explanations about whether each change is reversible or irreversible — connecting to First Nations Australians' (WESMOB) knowledge of natural materials.

Students explore Google Earth satellite views of landfill sites, recycling centres and natural material sources, comparing what sustainable and unsustainable material management looks like from above — sparking questions about why sustainably managing materials matters before investigations begin. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

ci: Patterns in the sky help us understand our place in the universe.
lo: Earth's place in the solar system; Observable patterns in the sky; Tools and methods we use to observe the sky

s.d: Students code an interactive solar system simulation in Scratch where planet sprites orbit the sun at different speeds and clicking each planet reveals facts from their Sky Journal observations, Moon Diary tracking, and learning about Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander constellations like the Emu in the Sky — visitors control the simulation with arrow keys to zoom in and out.

Students program Beebots to travel between planets on a scaled floor map of the solar system — entering the number of steps proportional to each planet's distance from the Sun. Mercury needs just a few presses; Neptune requires the maximum route. Students discover that the outer planets are vastly farther apart than they look in textbook diagrams, connecting the unit's inquiry into patterns in the sky with a physical, programmable experience of just how enormous the solar system is.

Students build a micro:bit 'Sky Pattern Tracker' that uses the light sensor to detect day vs night and displays animated moon phase icons on the LED screen matching the current phase from their Moon Diary — presenting their working instrument at a class science fair alongside their pattern-mapping charts of moon phases and sun positions.

Students build a motorised Lego Spike orrery with Earth and Moon models that rotate around a central sun, programming the speeds to demonstrate why the Moon takes a month to orbit Earth while Earth takes a year to orbit the Sun — connecting their Moon Diary observations to a physical working model.

Students use Google Earth to experience Earth from space — zooming out from their school to see all of Australia, then the whole planet, experiencing the shift in perspective from street level to orbital view. They compare what is visible at each zoom level (buildings, coastlines, cloud patterns, the curvature of the Earth) and connect this to how scientists use observation tools to understand our planet's place in the solar system. The experience makes abstract concepts of scale and perspective something students can navigate and control themselves. Note: this uses Google Earth's satellite and 3D globe view — not Street View. Students navigate the globe from above to explore landscapes and locations at scale. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

ci: Water is a limited resource that is essential to life.
lo: Unequal access to water; Communities affected by water access; Transferral of water from source to use; Responsibility as global citizens

s.d: Students film a green screen news broadcast as 'Water Watch Reporters' transporting themselves to drought-affected communities, water treatment plants and pristine water sources explored via Google Earth during the unit — delivering persuasive segments about water conservation responsibility as global citizens, referencing specific examples from their non-fiction reading and the curiosity board.

Students code an interactive 'Water Cycle Journey' simulation in Scratch where a water droplet sprite travels through evaporation, condensation, precipitation and collection — users click to add pollution or conservation actions that visibly change the water quality, connecting the water cycle stages to their home learning water-saving posters and class discussion about machines that move water from rivers to homes.

Students build a collaborative 'Water Around the World' Padlet — each group researching a different community and posting images, voice notes and written facts about their access to clean water. The class Padlet grows into a shared visual record of water inequality that students analyse together: which regions lack access, why, and what global citizens can do. The collaborative evidence base directly supports the unit's inquiry into unequal water access and responsibility as global citizens.

Students produce a short Canva Video documentary presenting their findings about how water travels from source to tap — narrating the journey through evaporation, condensation, precipitation and collection, incorporating diagrams and images gathered during the unit. The video becomes their summative explanation of the water cycle and the human systems that move water to homes, connecting the science content to the unit's focus on water as a limited resource and the global responsibility to manage it wisely.

Students use micro:bit moisture sensors to collect data on water usage in the school garden, tracking conservation efforts and connecting their findings to the unit's focus on responsible water management.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploring the past helps us to understand the present.
lo: Evidence and its significance in a place; Connections between the past and present within a place; Personal history shapes the present

s.d: Students produce an 'iMovie History Detectives' documentary combining footage from the Wonder Walk around Wesley Glen Waverley campus (plaques, statues, memorials, buildings) with photos from the Como House excursion, narrating what each piece of evidence reveals about the past — using slow-motion, zoom, and split-screen effects impossible without film production tools. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students film a green screen time-travel report where they 'visit' the Wesley campus in the past using the Old Wesley Picture Provocation as their backdrop, then switch to the present-day campus — performing as history reporters who compare what changed and what stayed the same, bringing the before-and-after evidence to life. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.
