Students code an animated 'Then and Now' timeline in Scratch Jr where tapping a landmark sprite transforms it from its historical version to its present-day appearance, narrating the changes with recorded voice — connecting their family tree exploration from Prep to a digital interactive that shows how personal histories shape the present.

Students use Google Maps Street View to explore Como House and the Glen Waverley area as it appears today, then compare it with historical photographs in Seesaw — annotating what has changed and what evidence of the past still exists in the built environment. The side-by-side comparison gives students a live archaeological lens: reading the present-day streetscape for traces of the past, connecting directly to the unit's inquiry into how evidence in a place helps us understand the connection between past and present.

Students use Google Earth to explore the Glen Waverley area and Como House virtually before their excursion — navigating the satellite view to identify the estate boundaries, compare the scale of the historic property against surrounding streets, and switch to Street View to walk the grounds in advance. The pre-visit virtual exploration sharpens their observation skills: students arrive at Como House already knowing the layout and carrying specific questions about the evidence of history they want to find in person, connecting directly to the unit's inquiry into how evidence in a place helps us understand connections between past and present. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

ci: Humans have a responsibility in their relationships to value the rights of others
lo: The difference between needs and wants; Everybody has rights and responsibilities; What is a respectful relationship

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Choose Your Response' scenario in Scratch Jr where a character faces a friendship dilemma (based on the Rainbow Fish discussion and role-play scenario cards), and the viewer taps to choose a respectful or disrespectful response — each choice triggers a different animated consequence showing how rights are upheld or broken.

Students build a Lego Spike 'Needs vs Wants Sorting Machine' that uses the colour sensor to detect items placed in front of it and motor-drives them down two different paths (need or want), then present their programmed machine to the class explaining how the sorting rules connect to the brainstorm activity about rights, needs and wants.

Students film a green screen news broadcast where they role-play reporters visiting different communities to investigate how rights are being upheld, transporting themselves to locations like schools, playgrounds and homes — connecting the scenario card role-plays to a professional broadcast format that couldn't exist without the technology. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students post examples of respectful and disrespectful digital scenarios to a class Padlet — drawing on the cybersafety content explored during the unit and their own experiences navigating online spaces. Presented anonymously, the collection is analysed together: which behaviours appear most often? Which are hardest to recognise? The collaborative curation turns individual observations into shared data, and the discussion that follows — about what patterns the class sees — is more powerful than any single student's example alone.

Students create an 'All About Me' page featuring drawings and voice recordings about everybody has rights and responsibilities, starting a personal book they will add to throughout the unit as their understanding of humans have a responsibility in their relationships to value the rights of others grows.

ci: Creativity allows people to express their understanding of the world.
lo: The ways people use technology to express creativity; How texts and images work together; How technology influences our creativity

s.d: Students produce a Stop Motion animation expressing their understanding of a concept from the unit, building on the skills taught in class where students learned to create Stop Motion clips and then taught their parents the technique. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students compose original soundtracks in GarageBand to accompany their Stop Motion animations in iMovie, exploring how music and sound effects enhance creative expression and convey meaning, connecting audio production to the multi-modal storytelling investigated throughout the unit.

Students build an interactive Scratch art gallery where the player walks a character through rooms, each containing a student-created digital artwork representing a different creative medium (painting, music, dance, digital). Clicking each artwork triggers an animation — a painting comes to life, a dancer moves across the stage, music notes float from instruments — and the player collects 'inspiration tokens' by answering how each medium expresses ideas differently, connecting to the unit's exploration of how technology expands creative possibilities beyond traditional media.

Students create a short video exploring how technology tools help people express ideas in new ways, documenting their Stop Motion creation process and reflecting on how technology influenced their creativity.

Students design an interactive digital story where text and images work together to convey meaning, extending their Book Creator experience from Year 2 and reflecting on their creative process using media technologies.

ci: Needs can be met with digital systems.
lo: What are digital systems; Ideas and information can be organised and shared in safe online environments; Being a responsible digital citizen

s.d: Students build an interactive 3D 'Digital Systems Tour' in CoSpaces where visitors walk through a virtual school and click on technology systems (library database, attendance system, communication tools) to see how digital systems meet community needs — presenting their virtual tour with narration explaining how ideas and information can be organised and shared in safe online environments.

Students build a Scratch simulation of a school library system where the player takes on the role of librarian — scanning books in using a barcode reader (clicking sprites), sorting them into the correct genre sections (dragging to shelves), and helping student characters find the right book by searching a catalogue. The game tracks how many books are correctly shelved and returned, with a timer adding pressure, demonstrating how digital systems use input, processing and output to meet real-world needs and what happens when parts of the system break down.

Students design a 3D model in Tinkercad of an innovative digital system that solves a school community need (automated book return, smart lunch ordering, digital lost-and-found), with labelled components showing how the system works — presenting their design alongside their research into how digital systems help meet community needs.

Students complete a quick class survey about their experience with what are digital systems — the teacher shares the live results on screen and the class discusses patterns, surprising answers and questions that emerge to drive the inquiry.

Students play a diagnostic Kahoot testing their assumptions about being a responsible digital citizen — the results reveal what the class already knows and where the biggest knowledge gaps are, helping the teacher shape the inquiry direction.

ci: Observing how materials change helps us make sense of the physical world.
lo: Observable properties of solids and liquids; How heating and cooling change state; Scientific thinking helps solve everyday problems

s.d: Students code an interactive 'States of Matter Explorer' in Scratch where clicking on a material sprite and dragging a temperature slider causes animated particles to change behaviour (tightly packed → flowing → spreading apart), with a data display showing the temperature at each transition point — connecting their micro:bit experiment data to a visual model.

Students produce an iMovie 'Mythbusters: Materials Edition' episode where they test a claim about how materials change (e.g. 'chocolate melts faster than butter'), filming their experiment with slow-motion replay of the state change moment, overlaying temperature data from their micro:bit readings, and delivering a verdict based on evidence.

Students build a Lego Spike automated heating/cooling experiment rig that uses the motor to stir a mixture and the temperature sensor to trigger an alert when a target temperature is reached — presenting their programmed experiment alongside data showing how heating and cooling change the state of materials.

Students program micro:bit temperature sensors to monitor material state changes during the unit's heating and cooling experiments — placing probes in ice water, room-temperature water, and heated water to collect real-time digital data rather than estimating by touch. The sensor transforms a subjective observation ('it feels cold') into a precise, comparable number, teaching students that scientific data requires reliable measurement tools. Results are recorded over time and graphed as a class, connecting directly to the unit's inquiry into how scientific thinking helps solve everyday problems by making the invisible visible.

Students collaboratively create a Kahoot quiz testing their classmates on states of matter concepts — writing questions based on their experiment observations about how heating and cooling change materials, turning their scientific knowledge into an interactive class challenge.

ci: The diversity of soils, rocks, and minerals shapes the way people value and use Earth's resources.
lo: Observable properties of soils, rocks and minerals; Earth's resources are vital and must be used responsibly; First Nations knowledge of soils and rocks

s.d: Students produce an Canva Video 'Earth's Resources' documentary featuring footage of their hands-on rock and soil investigations, Sphero terrain course experiments, and Google Earth geological explorations — delivering a persuasive argument about why Earth's resources must be used responsibly, with evidence from their classification data and First Nations perspectives.

Students design a 3D model in Tinkercad of a rock or mineral classification display stand with labelled compartments organised by observable properties (colour, texture, hardness, lustre), presenting their digital design as a museum-quality exhibit that could be 3D printed — connecting their hands-on investigation data to professional scientific presentation.

Students code an interactive 'Rock Cycle Journey' simulation in Scratch where a rock sprite transforms through igneous, sedimentary and metamorphic stages based on player choices (add heat, add pressure, erode), with First Nations knowledge of soils and rocks woven into the narration at each stage — creating an educational game visitors can play.

Students program micro:bits with light sensors to test different rock and mineral samples — measuring how much light they reflect to investigate the concept of lustre. They compare readings across their sample collection (metallic minerals like pyrite vs dull minerals like chalk) and build a data table of results. The sensor turns an observation task into a data collection experiment: students generate real numerical evidence for how the physical properties of soils, rocks and minerals differ, connecting digital science tools to the unit's geology inquiry.

Students compose a 'Geological Soundscape' in GarageBand — layering sounds to represent each layer of the Earth (deep percussion for the core, rumbling bass for the mantle, lighter tones for the crust) or to sonically represent the properties of rocks they studied (hard crystalline minerals as sharp plucked sounds, soft sedimentary rocks as muted tones, volcanic rock as crashing percussion). The composition requires students to translate geological properties into sound decisions, deepening their understanding through a completely different sensory medium.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Land and place are represented and valued in different ways.
lo: How Australia and neighbouring regions define places; Australia's states/territories have unique identities; First Nations connections to Country

s.d: Students design an annotated illustrated map in Canva of a place that holds meaning to them — their neighbourhood, a significant Australian location from the unit's inquiry, a First Nations Country, or a place from their own family history. Using Canva's design tools they add a title, compass rose, legend, and annotations explaining why each location on the map matters, applying the map conventions and spatial thinking explored throughout the unit. The five strongest class maps are submitted to the She Maps 'My Map, My World' national mapping competition (entries close 6 November) — giving the unit a real-world audience and purpose beyond the classroom.

Students design a 3D model in Tinkercad of a significant Australian landmark or place that represents First Nations Australians' deep spiritual, cultural and custodial connection to the land — presenting their digital design alongside research explaining why this place matters to different people and how the land holds identity and story.

Students program Spheros to navigate a floor map of Australia, travelling between states and territories while stopping at significant First Nations Country locations — at each stop they present what makes that place unique (geography, culture, Indigenous language group) using research from their Google Earth virtual tours, connecting coding and spatial reasoning to the unit's focus on how land and place are represented and valued in different ways. Note: this uses Google Earth's satellite and 3D globe view — not Street View. Students navigate the globe from above to explore landscapes and locations at scale. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

Students create a narrated virtual tour of significant Australian places in Google Earth — dropping pins at locations representing different state identities, First Nations Country boundaries, and geographic features studied during the unit. At each pin they record a voice annotation explaining why the place is significant, drawing on their research into how different communities value and represent land. The tour becomes a student-curated atlas reflecting the unit's central idea that land and place are represented and valued in different ways. Note: this uses Google Earth's satellite and 3D globe view — not Street View. Students navigate the globe from above to explore landscapes and locations at scale. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

Students design a multi-page presentation comparing how Australia's states and territories express unique identities through symbols, landmarks and culture, incorporating their two-dimensional map representations created during the Maths spatial integration.

ci: The choices and actions we make contribute to our health and wellbeing.
lo: What it means to be healthy; Choices and their impact on health; Actions to maintain a healthy mind and body

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Health Choices Simulator' in Scratch where players make daily decisions about food, exercise, sleep and screen time — each choice adjusts animated health meters for body and mind, demonstrating how the choices and actions we make contribute to our health and wellbeing through visible cause-and-effect feedback loops.

Students build a micro:bit 'Wellbeing Tracker' that uses the accelerometer to count steps and displays personalised activity targets on the LED screen, presenting their week-long PE athletics data alongside their device at a class health expo — demonstrating how technology can support actions to maintain a healthy mind and body.

Students compose a calming mindfulness soundscape in GarageBand exploring how music affects wellbeing, connecting audio creation to their investigation of how daily choices about mind and body health contribute to overall wellbeing, and sharing the composition as part of their healthy action strategies.

Students use Mentimeter live polling to gather anonymous class data on health and wellbeing choices weekly, creating real-time visualisations that reveal patterns in how the class's choices connect to physical and mental wellbeing — using the data to inform their Health Choices Simulator design.

Students create a personal 'Wellbeing Action Plan' book documenting healthy choices for mind and body, featuring their investigation findings from the two homework questions and one wondering they explored after the guest speaker presentations.

ci: Responding to different artistic expressions can promote thinking.
lo: Ways forms of expression convey messages; Creative and critical thinking skills; The language used to respond to The Arts

s.d: Students produce a podcast arts review episode — selecting one artwork, performance, or musical piece encountered during the unit and recording a structured critical response using the shared vocabulary developed throughout. The episode must cover: what artistic elements are present, what response the work triggered and why, how the creator's choices shaped that response, and one question the artwork left unanswered. Pairing two students who chose the same artwork but interpreted it differently creates a genuine debate format — the disagreement IS the learning, demonstrating the unit's central idea that responding to artistic expression promotes thinking. The podcast archives the class's collective critical vocabulary in a form parents and future students can actually listen to. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students create a narrated slideshow in Adobe Express Video Creator comparing how different artistic expressions they encountered during the unit — the Melbourne Museum's digital interactive, the provocations from visual art, music and drama — convey mood and meaning through different choices. Each slide presents one artwork with the student's spoken analysis: what artistic elements were used, what response it triggered, and how the creative choices shaped that response. The spoken critique format builds the unit's key skill of using the language of artistic response.

Students compose a 60-second musical response to a visual artwork in GarageBand — choosing instruments, tempo and mood that reflect their interpretation of the piece. Two students composing responses to the same artwork will almost always produce completely different music, which becomes the class discussion: what did you see that I didn't? The activity demonstrates the unit's central idea that responding to artistic expression promotes thinking, and that creative and critical thinking skills are inseparable — you can't compose meaningfully without analysing what you're responding to.

Students design an art response presentation analysing a chosen artwork using the consistent vocabulary developed during the unit (from the shared arts vocab folder), applying creative and critical thinking skills.

Students build a virtual 3D art gallery in CoSpaces displaying digital versions of the artworks studied during gallery walks, adding interactive annotations using the arts vocabulary developed throughout the unit, creating an immersive exhibition that extends their art response presentations.

ci: Communities create rules, laws and services to help people live together fairly and safely.
lo: Effective and influential leadership; Strengths that help leaders guide others; How leadership impacts communities

s.d: Students create a 'Leadership in Action' video showcasing their leadership speech (a favourite activity mentioned by students), incorporating footage from the Cross Campus Leadership Day team-building activities and their Seesaw self-reflection rubric responses.

Students produce a podcast interviewing peers about leadership qualities, connecting to the Visual Art exploration of art leaders Yayoi Kusama and Jean-Michel Basquiat and discussing how leadership exists in many fields. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students design a leadership profile presentation featuring the leader they researched, incorporating the ranking activity comparison (initial vs final understanding), the Kid President video analysis of leadership strengths and weaknesses, and insights from the Imogen Kane UN Youth Ambassador guest speaker on Teams.

Students use Freeform to build a visual concept map connecting different leadership examples explored during the unit — mapping how the election discussion, guest speaker visits and class observations reveal patterns in what makes leadership effective and influential.

Students use Minecraft Education to build a model community that demonstrates effective leadership in action — designing a village where different zones (council chamber, market, school, emergency services, green space) are connected by clear systems and accessible to all residents. Each leadership structure in the build must be justified: why is the council chamber central? Why is the emergency station near the main road? Students then deliberately introduce a leadership failure — removing the council chamber, blocking the market — and document what breaks down and why. The build externalises the unit's central idea: effective leadership isn't just a personal quality, it's a system of structures, access, and accountability that a community depends on.

ci: The design and use of products can have an impact on the natural world.
lo: How material properties determine their use; Material solutions for a sustainable future; How research solves problems in the natural world

s.d: Students conduct a school-wide rubbish audit — surveying lunchboxes across year levels and recording waste by material type (plastic, glass, metal, paper, organic) in Microsoft Excel. They build bar and pie charts directly from their tally data, then share their graphs on a class Padlet so every group can compare results. The data becomes direct evidence for the unit's central idea: product design choices create measurable, categorisable waste. Students use their charts to identify which material types could be reduced through better design decisions — connecting real numbers to the 'material solutions for a sustainable future' line of inquiry.

Students program a CoDrone EDU to perform an aerial survey of their sustainable product prototypes displayed around the classroom, capturing bird's-eye footage of each design — then present the drone footage alongside their Tinkercad 3D models and Lego Spike prototypes, explaining how their material choices solve an environmental problem for animals.

Students build a Lego Spike prototype of their sustainable product incorporating sensors and motors, testing the design against environmental criteria and comparing the engineering approach with their Tinkercad 3D digital prototype, connecting physical and digital design thinking.

Students code an interactive 'Sustainable Material Choices' simulation in Scratch where users select a product's material (plastic, glass, metal, bamboo, recycled) and see the environmental consequences play out — landfill fills up, carbon meter rises or falls, ocean pollution increases. The simulation requires students to translate their material research into logical if-then consequences, making the unit's central idea — that design and material choices impact the natural world — into a system students have to model and prove through code.

Students use Minecraft Education to design and build their sustainable product prototype in the context of the natural environment it's meant to protect — if the product addresses plastic waste in ocean habitats, students build the ocean ecosystem and place their solution within it; if it targets urban heat islands, they build the urban environment and demonstrate the cooling effect of their design. The Minecraft world makes the 'design and use of products can have an impact on the natural world' central idea directly visible: students build both the problem and the solution in the same environment, annotating the before/after difference with signs explaining the environmental impact data from their research. The build is presented alongside the physical Lego Spike prototype as a dual-format design pitch.

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: Diversity of First Nations people; Importance of community, family and traditions; Importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: Students produce a reflective documentary connecting their camp experience with the Indigenous elder to their research about how Country, language and community are significant — incorporating the persuasive speeches they wrote as their taking-action assessment.

DLP captures aerial footage of the camp location and local Glen Waverley environment, providing students with a bird's-eye view of Country that they compare with First Nations perspectives on the significance of land, connecting the aerial imagery to their Google Earth explorations of language group maps and significant locations. Note: this uses Google Earth's satellite and 3D globe view — not Street View. Students navigate the globe from above to explore landscapes and locations at scale. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

DLP captures 360° footage during the camp experience with the Indigenous elder — the nature walk, didgeridoo performance, and story sharing. Back in class, students explore the immersive footage on-screen, pausing to identify specific teachings about Country, language and community connection that they heard firsthand. The 360° format preserves the full sensory context of the elder's knowledge sharing, allowing students to revisit and deepen their reflections long after the camp experience — turning a one-off visit into ongoing reference material.

Students create a research book documenting the diversity of First Nations peoples and their connection to Country, integrating the pre-test visualisation activity where they drew their understanding of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities and researched the effects of First Fleet contact.

Students design a respectful presentation exploring the significance of Country to First Nations people, incorporating learning from the Indigenous elder at camp who led a nature walk, played the didgeridoo and shared stories, and connecting to their continued study of native Australian animals.

Students explore this lesson collection to experience stories and cultural practices of First Nations peoples from around the world, finding connections to the Woi wurrung and Boon wurrung knowledge shared by the Indigenous elder at camp. They compare what they observe with the nature walk teachings and story sharing, discussing what the Minecraft representation can and cannot capture about living culture. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/social-studies/Indigenous_Cultures_and_History

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to discoveries, opportunities and new understandings.
lo: Reasons for exploration; How people have explored over time; Consequences of exploration

s.d: Students create a documentary about historical exploration using green screen, incorporating research about convicts and the First Fleet and reflections from the CERES excursion where they enjoyed diorama making and hands-on exploration activities.

Students build an interactive 3D exploration scene in CoSpaces recreating a key moment from the Age of Exploration, adding historical details researched during library sessions and connecting the virtual environment to the physical dioramas created during CERES excursion activities.

Students design a multi-page presentation tracing a chosen explorer's journey, including motivations, discoveries and consequences — connecting to the historical narrative they wrote from a convict's point of view after researching convict experiences.

Students reconstruct a key Age of Exploration site in Minecraft Education — a historically researched recreation of a Portuguese caravel's deck, an Age of Sail trading port, or the campsite of Burke and Wills at a significant waypoint. Every build decision must be justified by evidence from the explorer research: if students place a crow's nest, they must explain its navigational function; if they build a supply store, they must account for what provisions the explorer's journal recorded. The finished build is toured by classmates who must identify three historically accurate features. The constraint of building within Minecraft's physical rules — materials must be structurally plausible, spaces must be navigable — forces students to think concretely about what exploration actually involved, connecting historical research to spatial and physical reality.

Students use Google Earth to trace the actual exploration routes studied during the unit — following Magellan's circumnavigation, Cook's Pacific voyage, and Burke and Wills' inland Australian route on the satellite globe. At each significant waypoint they drop a pin with an annotation explaining what the explorers discovered or encountered there and what consequence it had. Navigating the routes at true scale reveals what 'exploration' actually meant physically — months at sea, uncharted coastlines, and distances that are impossible to feel from a textbook map. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

ci: People's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life.
lo: Spiritual or religious beliefs; Similarities and differences of spiritual beliefs; Contributions of different religions to human diversity

s.d: Students build an interactive 3D 'World Beliefs Museum' in CoSpaces where visitors walk through themed rooms for each religion studied, clicking on artefact and symbol objects to trigger recorded explanations about spiritual beliefs, traditions and contributions to human diversity — connecting their Think-Pair-Share research to an immersive virtual exhibition.

Students produce a podcast episode investigating how beliefs shape communities — each episode focuses on one religion or spiritual tradition studied during the unit (Christianity, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, First Nations spirituality), presenting its core beliefs, key practices, and contribution to human diversity. Crucially, students must identify at least one significant similarity with another tradition covered in the unit, building the class's shared understanding that diversity of belief doesn't preclude common ground. The podcast format requires students to explain beliefs clearly and respectfully for a general audience — a higher-order task than summarising facts on a poster, and one that directly practises the unit's key skill of using the language of interfaith dialogue. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students design a comparative presentation exploring similarities and differences between religions studied during the unit, incorporating observations from the Uniting Church visit, the Bunjil art installation, and the chapel experience with Steven Langley's cello performance and meditation.

Students use Freeform to create an individual concept map exploring how their own values and spiritual or religious beliefs connect to those of people from different traditions studied during the unit. Starting with their own centre, they branch out to connect beliefs they share and beliefs that differ, mapping similarities across Christianity, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism and First Nations spirituality. The visual comparison reveals that diversity of belief doesn't mean absence of common ground — directly interrogating the unit's central idea that beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life.

Students use Google Earth to take a virtual pilgrimage tour — navigating to significant religious and spiritual sites worldwide: the Vatican in Rome, Mecca in Saudi Arabia, the Ganges River in Varanasi, Uluru in Central Australia, and Buddhist temples in Japan. At each site they discuss what the architecture, location and surrounding landscape reveals about the belief system it belongs to, connecting geographical context to the unit's inquiry into how spiritual beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life. Note: this uses Google Earth's satellite and 3D globe view — not Street View. Students navigate the globe from above to explore landscapes and locations at scale. At St Kilda Rd where Pico VR is available, this Google Earth exploration can be delivered as a fully immersive VR experience — students step inside the environment rather than navigating it from a screen.

ci: People design for purpose.
lo: How design thinking creates in various ways; Design thinking meets community needs; Effective ways to engage an audience

s.d: Students build a Lego Spike functional prototype of their design thinking product, programming it in Scratch to perform its core function — a mechanism that moves, sorts, measures or responds to input. The dual build tests whether their design actually works as intended, not just on paper. Students present the working prototype alongside their Scratch code, explaining how the programming decisions they made shaped the physical behaviour, connecting the unit's LO on how design thinking meets community needs to a real, testable outcome.

Students create a pitch video for their design explaining how it engages an audience and meets a community need, applying the persuasive presentation and sequencing skills from the literacy integration.

Students design a 3D prototype of their product using the full design thinking process (empathise, define, ideate, prototype, test) to meet a specific community need, iterating based on peer feedback.

Students explore drone technology as a design-for-purpose case study, programming CoDrone EDU to perform tasks that meet a specific community need (surveillance for safety, delivery for accessibility), connecting the drone's engineered purpose to their design thinking investigation of how products are designed to serve audiences.

Students integrate micro:bits into their design project as sensors or interactive elements, collecting data to inform design decisions and connecting technology to the ethical and practical considerations in their design process.

ci: Economic systems are an integral part of human society.
lo: Wealth is more than money; Responsibilities when managing income; Income is earned and managed in many ways

s.d: Students produce a podcast episode in Adobe Express discussing how economic systems work and responsible financial decision-making, drawing on their Banqer simulation experience and class presentations about managing income. Each episode focuses on a specific economic concept students genuinely wrestled with during Banqer — tax, unexpected expenses, income inequality — presenting what they learned through the simulation alongside their own analysis. The podcast format requires students to explain economic ideas to a general audience, consolidating understanding while practising the communication skills the unit demands. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students create a personal budget spreadsheet in Microsoft Excel using formulas to track income, expenses and savings goals — modelling the financial decisions they encountered in Banqer but with the added complexity of creating the tracking system from scratch. They enter realistic income scenarios from the unit's discussion about how income is earned and managed, then add unexpected expenses (medical costs, car repairs) to test whether their budget survives real-world pressure. The exercise makes the abstract concept of financial management tangible through the discipline of formula-driven data.

Students design an economic literacy guide explaining different forms of wealth and responsible income management, incorporating their experience managing money in Banqer where they applied for jobs, side hustles and made financial decisions.

Students code an interactive budget simulation in Scratch where users make a series of financial decisions — choosing how to allocate income between needs, wants, savings and giving. Each decision triggers a consequence that updates a running balance on screen, and students must program the logic for both responsible and irresponsible financial choices. Building the simulation requires them to define exactly what a good financial decision is in code, which is a far more rigorous form of understanding than describing it in words.

Students design and conduct a survey in Microsoft Forms investigating how classmates understand wealth beyond money — asking about what they value most, what 'enough' means to them, and whether they'd trade income for other things like time or purpose. After collecting responses, they analyse the auto-generated charts to identify patterns and compare their class data with the economic frameworks studied. The survey design process is as important as the data: students must translate abstract economic concepts into measurable questions, which demands genuine conceptual clarity.

ci: The interactions between living things, materials, and light enable us to make decisions about the world
lo: How structural features and adaptations help living things survive; How weathering and erosion cause change to Earth's surface; How light is used in science and technology

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: The need for conservation; Community values inform management of the natural environment; Ways environmental stewardship can drive change

s.d: DLP captures aerial footage of the Alex Wilkie Nature Reserve and surrounding urban development, giving students a bird's-eye view of how community interactions physically shape the local environment. Students compare the drone footage with Google Earth satellite imagery to analyse green space patterns, development density and biodiversity zones — annotating printed aerial stills to identify conservation areas and development pressures. The dual aerial perspective builds the visual evidence base for their stewardship action proposals.

Students produce a podcast episode pitching their conservation stewardship proposal to a specific decision-maker — the local Monash Council environment officer, the school's grounds team, or the Alex Wilkie Nature Reserve committee. Each episode opens with the problem (framed with micro:bit sensor data and drone footage observations from class), presents a specific actionable proposal, and closes with a direct call to action. Designing the episode for a named real-world audience changes the precision students bring to their evidence — 'the reserve needs protection' becomes 'sensor data shows temperature differential of X degrees between the reserve's edge and urban boundary, suggesting habitat fragmentation.' The podcast is submitted to the relevant council or school body as a genuine act of environmental stewardship. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students program micro:bits to collect environmental data (temperature, light, moisture) around the school and at the Alex Wilkie Nature Reserve, using findings to support their conservation action proposals with evidence.

DLP captures 360° footage at the Alex Wilkie Nature Reserve during the excursion. Back in class, students explore the immersive footage on-screen to identify environmental features, locate evidence of human impact, and compare biodiversity between the reserve's natural zones and the surrounding urban development. The 360° perspective reveals landscape-scale connections — how green corridors link habitats, where development edges push against conservation land — providing visual evidence students cite directly in their environmental stewardship action plan presentations.

Students use Minecraft Education to design a conservation management plan for the Alex Wilkie Nature Reserve — building a to-scale Minecraft model of the reserve and surrounding urban development using aerial drone footage and Google Earth satellite imagery from class as their spatial reference. They mark existing green corridors, biodiversity zones, and development pressure points in the build, then design and construct their proposed stewardship interventions: wildlife crossing bridges, native vegetation corridors, stormwater filtration zones, community access paths that don't disturb habitat. Each intervention is annotated with a sign explaining the ecological rationale, drawing on the micro:bit sensor data and excursion observations. The finished Minecraft plan is presented to the class as a conservation proposal — the same proposal, but built rather than drawn.

Students explore the Sustainability City world and analyse how sustainable systems — solar energy grids, composting facilities, green transport, responsible forestry — function as interconnected community decisions. They then adapt one district to model a conservation intervention relevant to the Alex Wilkie Nature Reserve research, presenting their Minecraft design alongside the micro:bit sensor data and drone footage gathered during class. Access the world and lesson plans at education.minecraft.net/resources/science-subject-kit/sustainability

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: The discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations.
lo: Significance of resource discovery in development of societies; Impact of migration on cultural, social and economic growth; Exploration and settlement effects on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Migration Impact Timeline' in Scratch where clicking on era sprites (pre-colonial, Gold Rush, post-war, modern) triggers animated data visualisations showing how migration shaped Australian communities — connecting their Microsoft Excel data analysis to an interactive experience visitors can explore.

Students film first-person Gold Rush accounts using green screen with historical goldfields backgrounds, performing as characters from different migration waves to bring primary source stories to life, integrating the footage into their iMovie documentary about how resource discovery impacted Australia's growth.

Students produce a podcast episode examining the impact of migration on Australia's development — each episode takes a specific migration wave (Gold Rush, post-war Ten Pound Poms, refugee intake, Pacific Islander workers) and analyses how that wave shaped Australian society culturally, economically, and politically. Students must include at least one First Nations perspective on the changes each wave brought, situating migration not as a story of arrival but of transformation — including for those who were already here. The podcast format forces precise, evidence-based argument: students cannot hide behind footage or music; their analysis must carry the episode. Episodes are compiled into a class 'Migration History' series. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

DLP captures 360° footage at Sovereign Hill or a Gold Rush heritage site during the excursion, preserving the immersive historical environment for classroom revisitation. Students explore the footage back at school to gather visual evidence — identifying the physical layout of the goldfields, the diversity of people present, the tools and technology of the era — and annotate screenshots as primary source evidence for their research into how the discovery of resources impacted the growth and development of nations. The immersive footage turns a one-day excursion into an ongoing reference resource.

Students choose a historical character from the Gold Rush era — a Chinese miner, an Indigenous Australian whose land was disrupted, a ship passenger on the £10 Pom scheme, a Eureka Stockade participant — and record their first-person account in Adobe Express Character Animator. The character animates to their voice with a historically appropriate background, creating a perspective piece that goes beyond the written account and brings the impact of migration and resource discovery to life through embodied storytelling.

s.t: Minecraft Education (World Cities and Civilizations + Indigenous Cultures and History)
s.d: Students explore 'World Cities and Civilizations' to experience how resource discoveries shaped historical settlements, then move to 'Indigenous Cultures and History' to understand what those same discoveries meant for communities already living there. The paired experience grounds the unit's central idea — that resource discovery impacts the growth of nations — in both coloniser and First Nations perspectives. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/social-studies/World_Cities_and_Civilizations

ci: Managing change requires acceptance, knowledge and strategies.
lo: Establishing, managing and maintaining relationships; Managing behaviours in digital situations; Managing changes associated with puberty

s.d: Students produce a podcast episode in Adobe Express discussing strategies for managing one of the three change areas explored during the unit — establishing and maintaining relationships, managing behaviours in digital situations, or navigating puberty. Each episode features a scenario, a 'what not to do' analysis, and evidence-based strategies drawn from the unit's health content. The podcast format requires students to be precise and persuasive rather than just informative, and the finished episodes become a class resource bank that younger students could genuinely use. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students create a cyber safety awareness video in Canva Video demonstrating one responsible digital behaviour explored during the unit — managing online relationships, protecting personal information, or responding to cyberbullying. Each group chooses a real scenario from class discussion, scripts a 'what actually happens' version and a 'what you should do' version, and presents both as a split narrative. The contrast between the two versions makes the stakes of digital decision-making concrete, turning the unit's exploration of managing behaviours in digital situations into a student-produced teaching resource.

Students compose a calming audio toolkit in GarageBand with personalised soundscapes and guided breathing exercises, connecting music creation to the wellbeing and change management strategies explored during the relationship, cyber safety and resilience sessions throughout the unit.

Students design a comprehensive Canva Presentation wellbeing guide covering the three change areas explored during the unit — relationships, digital behaviour and puberty. Each section includes a real scenario, common misconceptions students identified during class discussions, evidence-based strategies, and local support resources. The guide is designed for a specific audience: Year 4 students preparing to transition. Designing for a known audience with real needs forces students to apply the unit's content with precision rather than generality, making it both a summative assessment and a genuinely useful artefact.

Students code an interactive cyber safety scenario in Scratch where users navigate a social media situation — receiving a suspicious message, seeing a friend post something hurtful, or being asked to share personal information. Each choice branches to a consequence: safe choices lead to positive outcomes, risky choices show realistic impacts. The branching logic means students must anticipate consequences before coding them, which develops the exact reasoning process the unit aims to build: thinking ahead about the real-world effects of digital decisions.

lo: How media targets audiences; Techniques used to engage and persuade; Critical evaluation of media messages

ci: Scientists undertake experimentation to confirm, contest and create new theories.
lo: The scientific method; How experiments lead to new understandings; The relationship between planets and the Sun; Energy transfer and transformation in circuits

s.d: Students use the micro:bit's light sensor to build an energy monitoring experiment — programming it to measure how long classroom lights are left on unnecessarily and logging that data over multiple sessions. They then interpret the data on a computer to identify patterns and calculate wasted energy, connecting the coding and sensor work directly to the unit's LO on energy transfer and transformation in circuits. The experiment applies the scientific method the unit centres on: forming a hypothesis about school energy habits, collecting real sensor data, analysing it for patterns, and presenting evidence-based recommendations — a genuine science investigation made possible only because the micro:bit can automate what a human observer couldn't sustain.

Students design a 3D scale model of the solar system in Tinkercad using data from their playdough planet-modelling activity, with accurate relative sizes and labelled orbital paths — presenting their digital design alongside their CoSpaces virtual solar system to demonstrate the relationship between planets and the Sun from both engineering and immersive perspectives.

Students code an interactive 'Squishy Circuits Lab' in Scratch where visitors build virtual circuits by dragging conductor and insulator sprites into a circuit diagram — the program tests whether the circuit is complete and demonstrates energy transfer with animated electron flow, connecting their hands-on playdough circuit investigations to a shareable digital simulation.

Students use Minecraft Education to build a to-scale model of the solar system — using the playdough planet-size comparisons from class as their sizing guide and programming NPC characters to deliver accurate facts about each planet's composition, orbital period, and relationship to the Sun when interacted with. The build makes the unit's central challenge tangible: representing true relative distances in Minecraft requires students to apply the same mathematical reasoning as professional orrery designers, and the result makes viscerally clear why the solar system is mostly empty space. Students compare their Minecraft model with the Tinkercad design, analysing what each medium can and cannot represent accurately — directly connecting to the unit's inquiry into how scientists use models and simulations to understand phenomena they cannot observe directly.

Students build an interactive 3D solar system in CoSpaces using data from their playdough scale planet models, creating a virtual space that visitors can explore with accurate planet sizes and distances, extending their science poster and investigation journal into an immersive digital experience.

ci: The influences on our consumer choices reveal the interdependence between human and natural systems and call for responsible action toward just and sustainable futures.
lo: Influences on consumer choices (function); How consumer decisions impact living things and their habitats (causation); Alternative models of consumerism that honour a fair and sustainable future (perspective)

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Neuromarketing Simulator' in Scratch where the player scrolls through a simulated social media feed — product ads appear and the player must identify the specific technique being used before they can scroll past (colour psychology, scarcity language, celebrity endorsement, algorithm-driven repetition). Getting it wrong triggers the 'you've been influenced' animation and costs points. Building the simulator requires students to translate everything they learned from the Cadbury case study and the neuromarketing video into coded logic — they can only program a manipulation tactic once they genuinely understand how it works, making the analysis far more rigorous than a worksheet response.

Students design and distribute a school-wide consumer survey in Microsoft Forms investigating what actually drives purchasing decisions at GW — peer pressure, advertising, price, sustainability, brand loyalty, packaging. They collect responses across multiple year levels, then import the raw data into Excel to build charts comparing how different age groups are influenced. The patterns they find — whether Year 4s are more brand-loyal than Year 6s, whether sustainability matters more to older students — become the evidence base for their LOI 1 investigation and the data they're required to include in their multimodal persuasive presentation. The survey design process also forces students to operationalise the unit's key concepts as measurable questions.

Students compose and record their taking-action piece in GarageBand — a spoken word poem, rap or consumer manifesto delivered over a layered backing track they produce themselves. The literary devices the planner requires (metaphor, alliteration, simile, idiom) are woven into lyrics that use the same neuromarketing techniques they analysed in the Cadbury study, but now flipped to promote ethical consumption: tempo to create urgency, warm instrument choices to build trust, repetition to lodge the message. The deliberate use of manipulation tactics they've critiqued — now deployed for a just cause — is the unit's central tension made audible.

Students build an interactive 3D 'Ethical Consumer Museum' in CoSpaces structured around The Story of Stuff framework — visitors walk through rooms representing each stage of their chosen product's supply chain (extraction, production, distribution, consumption, disposal), with clickable objects revealing the environmental and ethical data from their investigation. One room displays the advertising techniques they analysed; another houses the worker conditions research; a final room presents their sustainable alternative and the evidence supporting it. The museum IS the Part 1 group presentation — other classes and parents can be invited to explore it rather than watching a PowerPoint, giving the unit's research a genuine public audience.

Students choose a character from within their product's supply chain — a cocoa farmer in Ghana, a garment worker, a delivery driver, a coral reef species affected by packaging waste — and record their first-person account in Adobe Express Character Animator. The character animates to their voice with a contextually appropriate background; students add music that reflects the emotional tone of the testimony. The script draws directly on the ethics research they conducted, the child labour case study, and the Story of Stuff production-chain analysis. The class collection of animated supply chain voices — each connected to a different product — becomes a powerful exhibition piece demonstrating LOI 2 through embodied, first-person testimony that no report or poster can replicate.

Students analyse the Sustainability City world as a case study in how consumer and community choices shape environmental outcomes — connecting to the unit's central idea about consumer interdependence with natural systems. They identify which systems reflect responsible consumption and redesign one district to model an ethical consumer alternative linked to the product chain they investigated. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/science-subject-kit/sustainability

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human needs drive the development of nations.
lo: How human needs drive national development; Role of migration in nation building; Impacts of development on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students produce a documentary about Australia's nation-building journey, incorporating perspectives from First Nations people before European migration, the Melbourne Museum excursion observations, and the Seesaw summative assessment showcasing three key concepts from the unit.

Students code an interactive 'Nation Building Timeline' in Scratch where visitors click on era sprites (pre-colonial, early settlement, gold rush, federation, modern) to see animated demonstrations of how human needs drove development — featuring push-pull migration factors, Indigenous perspectives, and notable Australian voices from their research.

Students film first-person historical accounts using green screen, performing as figures from different eras to demonstrate how human needs drove the development of nations, combining the footage with their research into an engaging documentary that brings history to life.

Students compose era-appropriate soundtracks in GarageBand for their historical documentaries, selecting instruments and styles that reflect different time periods and cultures studied during the unit, connecting music production to their research on how civilisations developed in response to human needs.

Students choose a character in Adobe Express Character Animator to represent a voice from the nation-building migrations studied during the unit — a post-war European migrant, a refugee, an economic migrant, or an Indigenous Australian reflecting on the changes brought by development. They record their first-person account with a historically appropriate background, the character animating automatically to their narration. The class collection of animated migration perspectives — voiced in first person across different roles and time periods — makes the human dimension of nation building visible in a way timelines and reports cannot.

s.t: Minecraft Education (Indigenous Cultures and History + World Cities and Civilizations)
s.d: Students explore both world collections to experience how human needs drove the development of nations from two very different perspectives — those building new settlements, and those already living on the land those settlements displaced. The paired experience anchors the unit's three lines of inquiry — how human needs drive development, the role of migration, and the impacts on Indigenous populations — in immersive environments. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/social-studies/Indigenous_Cultures_and_History

ci: People recognise important events through celebrations and traditions.
lo: The traditions of our families and communities; How and why people celebrate; The similarities and differences between celebrations

s.d: Students produce a 'Celebrations Around the World' TV show — each group films a segment about a different celebration explored during the unit (Chinese New Year Lion Dancing, Hanukkah, Diwali, Mid-Autumn Festival), dressing in costumes, demonstrating the dances learned in class, and showing the cultural artefacts and food they explored. The segments are compiled into a class TV show with student hosts introducing each celebration.

Students create a 'Celebration Sounds' composition in GarageBand that layers music and sounds from different cultural celebrations explored during the unit — drums for Chinese New Year, Simi Yadech rhythms, Mexican Hat Dance beats, Chimes of Dunkirk melody. The composition builds as each culture's sounds are added, demonstrating that celebrations share common elements (music, rhythm, joy) even when they sound different — a musical Venn diagram the class can hear.

Students code a 'Dance Around the World' interactive animation in Scratch Jr where a character travels to different countries and performs a cultural dance at each stop — moving to the Simi Yadech steps in one scene, the Mexican Hat Dance in another, the Chimes of Dunkirk in a third. Each scene has a different backdrop and the character's movements match the dance, creating a coded world tour of celebrations that plays automatically.

Students program Cubetto to visit stations on a ‘Dance Around the World’ floor mat, stopping at each country to explain the cultural dance they learned — Simi Yadech, The Chimes of Dunkirk, Mexican Hat Dance — connecting coding sequences to the dance steps performed and added to their classroom ‘Dance Around the World’ board.

Students use Google Maps Street View to virtually visit the locations where celebrations from the unit take place — exploring the streets of China during New Year decorations, finding Diwali lights in an Indian neighbourhood, or spotting NAIDOC banners around Australia. The Street View journey puts students at ground level inside the cultural context of each celebration, building geographic and cultural awareness that supports the unit's inquiry into why celebrations matter to different communities around the world.

ci: We all have roles and responsibilities in the community
lo: Roles people play; Responsibilities in the community; How working together helps everyone

s.d: Students produce a 'Thank You Heroes' video — each student films a short message to a school community member whose role they investigated, explaining what they learned about that person's responsibilities and why their role matters. The teacher compiles the messages into a video that is actually delivered to the staff members, giving the students' work real purpose and demonstrating that understanding roles leads to appreciation and action.

Students dress up in costumes representing different community roles (firefighter, librarian, doctor, teacher) and film themselves in front of the green screen placed in the relevant setting — a library, a fire station, a hospital, a classroom. Each student explains their responsibilities and why working together helps everyone, connecting to the Essential Agreement they created and the Firefighters' visit discussion. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code a 'Busy Community' animation in Scratch Jr showing different community helpers performing their roles in sequence — the teacher opens the classroom, the librarian shelves books, the crossing guard stops traffic, the gardener waters plants. When all helpers have performed, the school 'comes alive' with movement everywhere, demonstrating visually that a community only works when everyone plays their part.

Students use Canva Video to create a 'Community Helpers' visual tour — filming each staff member at their actual workplace (the librarian at the shelves, the grounds crew on the oval, the office manager at the front desk) and recording a narrated voiceover explaining what each person does and why the school depends on them. Each clip becomes one chapter in a class documentary that is shown at assembly. Canva Video's accessible editing tools mean students can build this independently; the visual format captures the physical spaces and people far more vividly than audio alone, giving families a genuine window into the hidden workforce that keeps the school running.

Students program Beebots to navigate a school map visiting different staff members’ locations (classroom, library, office, playground), explaining each person’s role at every stop — building on the Year 4 buddy discussions about staff roles and the books about starting school (Starting School by Jane Godwin, Possum Goes to School, First Day by Andrew Daddo).

lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

ci: Human actions change the environment.
lo: How people interact with different environments; The effect people have on the environment; Ways in which people can take action and care for the environment

s.d: Students film a 'Save Our Oceans' news report using green screen — one reporter is 'underwater' at the Great Barrier Reef (healthy coral backdrop) explaining how the reef looks when it's cared for, then the background switches to bleached coral and the reporter explains what happens when humans don't take action. Students play both the reporters AND the sea creatures (with costumes from their Visual Arts marine animal project), creating a documentary that transports them inside the environment. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code a two-scene 'Before and After' animation in Scratch Jr — Scene 1 shows a healthy environment (animals moving, plants growing, sun shining), Scene 2 shows the same environment after harmful human actions (animals disappearing, plants wilting, rubbish appearing). A third scene shows the student's proposed action to fix it. The animation becomes a digital version of the 'before-and-after' thinking used throughout the unit, but one that moves, sounds and performs itself.

Students produce a 'Three Sea Creature Facts' video (connecting to their Student Led Conference preparation) but elevated — each student films their presentation with their ceramic/painted marine animal artwork as a prop, the teacher adds underwater footage and sound effects in iMovie, and slow-motion captures of the Prep Tunnel underwater exhibition bring the display to life on screen. The finished video becomes the actual SLC presentation resource parents watch. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students build an ‘Environment Path’ using colour tiles representing different natural environments (blue = marine, yellow = desert, green = bushland), programming Indi to travel through each environment while explaining the living things found there and one human action that could help or harm it — connecting to the environment provocation bottles (sand/water, leaves/sticks).

Students photograph their collaborative Prep Tunnel underwater exhibition and the ceramic, painted and drawn marine animals they created in Visual Arts, recording voice explanations about each sea creature’s features and why caring for the ocean environment matters — connecting to picture books Commotion in the Ocean and Ella’s Adventures in the Barrier Reef.

ci: To be healthy, people’s basic needs must be met.
lo: How our brain is growing and changing; How our emotions, behaviours and actions promote relationships; How we care for our bodies

s.d: Students create a 'Brain Break' audio toolkit in GarageBand — each student records a guided breathing exercise or calm-down strategy over soothing background music (balloon breathing with gentle chimes, wall push with steady drums, calm counting with soft piano). The class compiles them into a playlist that the teacher plays during actual brain breaks, making the students' work a real classroom resource used every day — not just an assignment to be marked.

Students perform their 'Healthy Choices' demonstration in front of the green screen — one student is transported inside a kitchen demonstrating healthy food choices, another is placed on a sports field showing active play, another is in a bedroom demonstrating sleep routines. Each performance demonstrates one of the seven Healthy Lifestyle Jigsaw elements in a real-world setting they couldn't physically visit during class. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code a 'Healthy Day' animation in Scratch Jr showing a character's routine from waking up to bedtime — eating brain food at recess (not sometimes foods), choosing active play at lunch, using a Zones of Regulation strategy when feeling frustrated, going to bed on time. The animation demonstrates the connection between daily choices and wellbeing, playing automatically when the green flag is pressed.

Students build a ‘Healthy Choices’ colour-tile path where each colour represents one of the seven healthy lifestyle elements from the Jigsaw (green = exercise, blue = sleep, yellow = healthy food, purple = mind health), programming Indi to follow the path while explaining how each element meets a basic need for staying healthy.

Students photograph their brain food recess choices and use drawing tools to sort foods into ‘always’ and ‘sometimes’ categories, recording voice explanations about why some foods fill our batteries and others could make them overflow — building on the Nude Foods vs Processed Foods sorting activity and the Cyber Safety lesson ‘Keeping the Balance’.

ci: People express their emotions through the way they communicate.
lo: Different ways to communicate emotions; How art, music and movement express feelings; Understanding others' expressions through body language and facial cues

s.d: Students produce a short video showcasing their Emotion Exhibition (where they sculpted partners into emotion poses), adding narration explaining which emotions are being communicated through body language and facial expressions — with their Grow and Glow digital portfolio reflection.

Students create an 'Expressing Emotions' portfolio featuring their Frida Kahlo portrait (recreating her nonchalant expression), their Emotion Exhibition photographs (sculpting partners to show emotions through body language and facial expression), and their sports team jumper design showing how colour, patterns and mascots communicate identity.

Students complete the 'Thinking Thoughts and Feeling Feelings' assessment task from the We Thinkers! series, demonstrating their ability to recognise social cues and reframe situations to avoid uncomfortable feelings — building on the Kimochi Emotions vocabulary sheet and the Mona Lisa facial expression activity.

Students create a soundscape representing different weather-inspired emotions, connecting to the music listening activities where they analysed how composers use tempo, dynamics and instruments to communicate feelings (exploring works and reflecting on what feelings the music evoked).

Students contribute to a class 'Emotion Exhibition' Padlet — posting a photo of their Frida Kahlo-inspired self-portrait alongside a voice note explaining which emotion they chose to express, which artistic elements (colour, line, expression) they used to show it, and how the artwork compares to communicating the same emotion through music or movement. The Padlet becomes the digital version of the physical exhibition the unit builds toward, extending the audience beyond the classroom and inviting parents to engage with the students' learning.

ci: Systems are involved in preparing products for consumers.
lo: Where products come from; The systems products go through; How people choose their products

s.d: Students produce a 'From Farm to Fork' documentary using stop-motion animation — filming the transformation of a raw material into a finished product using real food items and toy props (a toy cow, flour, bread dough, a finished sandwich). Each step of the system is captured frame by frame, creating a visual production line that shows the entire journey from raw material to consumer product. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students code a 'Product Journey' animation in Scratch Jr showing the system a product goes through — from farm to factory to truck to shop to home. Characters and backdrops change at each stage, the product transforms (wheat becomes flour becomes bread), and the student narrates the sequencing vocabulary (consume, process, transportation, storage) built throughout the unit. The system plays automatically, demonstrating the whole chain.

Students film themselves as 'product reporters' transported via green screen to each stage of a product's journey — standing in a wheat field, inside a factory, on a delivery truck, at a supermarket shelf. They explain what happens at each stage using the vocabulary built throughout the unit, creating a news-style report that takes the audience on a journey through places they couldn't physically visit in one lesson. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students use Google Earth to follow the journey of a t-shirt — zooming from a cotton farm in Queensland to a factory in China to a shop on Chapel Street. At each stop they screenshot the location and the teacher asks: 'What happens to the product here?' — building geographic understanding of the system that connects raw materials to the clothes they're wearing, before the class reads clothing labels to discover where their own clothes come from. Note: this uses Google Earth's satellite and 3D globe view — not Street View. Students navigate the globe from above to explore landscapes and locations at scale.

Students photograph clothing labels to identify where products come from and the materials they're made of, recording voice explanations about the travel involved in production and distribution — connecting to the George the Farmer cotton video and the Clickview 'War on Waste' and 'Farm to Table' series.

ci: Weather affects our lives in different ways.
lo: Types of weather conditions; How weather impacts our lives (clothing, activities, food, feelings); Why weather changes (seasons, patterns)

s.d: Students produce a professional weather forecast video — each group reports on a different season (including Kulin Nation seasons), using the daily weather graph data they collected as evidence, displaying their findings on a classroom weather map, and delivering forecasts with dramatic weather footage filmed around the school. The format transforms their data collection into a broadcast that uses scientific vocabulary and Chinese weather characters learned in class.

Students perform their frozen tableaux representing seasonal activities in front of the green screen — placed in weather-appropriate environments (a snowfield for winter activities, a beach for summer, an autumn forest, a rainy day). Each group narrates how the weather affects clothing, food, activities and feelings in that season, creating an immersive seasonal experience that brings the tableau performance to life in ways costumes alone couldn't achieve. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code a 'Weather Wardrobe' game in Scratch Jr — a character wakes up and checks the weather (sun, rain, snow, wind), then the player must drag the right clothing and accessories onto the character before they go outside. Wrong choices lead to animated consequences (shivering in rain without a coat, sweating in snow gear on a sunny day), teaching weather-life connections through an interactive game the student coded themselves.

Students use micro:bit temperature and light sensors to collect daily weather data, building on the daily weather graph activity and connecting digital data collection to the Bureau of Meteorology research they conducted on iPads.

Students use Seesaw to document their season observation journals over the term — photographing and annotating evidence of each season as it changes (bare trees, blossoms, leaf colours, rain vs sunshine), building a longitudinal record that spans the whole unit. The final entry compares all four seasons: students record a voice explanation identifying which weather patterns they observed, how each season affected what they wore and did, and what they predict will come next. The portfolio becomes the unit's summative evidence of sustained scientific observation.

ci: Living things have structures that allow them to survive in different environments.
lo: The physical structure of plants and bees; How features support survival in different environments; Human impact on living things

s.d: Students produce a 'Garden Investigators' documentary — filming close-up footage of the broad bean experiment at each growth stage (comparing beans grown in light, dark and varied water), the bee sculptures alongside their scientific mirror-image drawings, and the plants observed at the Royal Botanic Gardens. Slow-motion captures pollination in the school garden, and student narrators explain how structural features enable survival. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students film themselves 'shrunk to the size of a bee' using green screen — transported inside a giant flower where they can see pollen up close, then flying through the school garden at bee-level. They report on what a bee's day looks like, explaining pollination, the roles bees play within their community, and why flowers and plants support a healthy planet — an impossible perspective made possible by the tech.
