The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code a 'Bee's Journey' animation in Scratch Jr showing a bee visiting three flowers, collecting pollen at each stop, then returning to the hive. The animation demonstrates pollination as a system — without the bee's journey, the flowers can't reproduce. Each scene shows the bee interacting with a different plant, connecting the coded sequence to the real pollination process observed during the unit's garden investigations.

Students use micro:bit light and moisture sensors to measure conditions in different parts of the school garden — comparing sunny spots with shady spots, dry soil with damp soil. They record readings on a class chart and connect the data to where they found the most living things, discovering that plants and insects thrive in specific conditions — science data collection that couldn't happen without the sensors.

Students create a 'Bee Scientist' page in Book Creator featuring a photo of their clay bee sculpture alongside their scientific mirror-image drawing, with labels identifying the body parts (3 sections, 6 legs, wings, antennae) and a voice recording explaining the roles bees play within their community — building the observation and documentation skills practised throughout the unit.

Students explore the 'Build with Bees' world — developed with the American Beekeeping Federation — investigating bee habitats, pollination routes, and the structural features that make bees essential to ecosystems. They discover how bees' physical structures enable survival and design bee-friendly garden additions within Minecraft that could be replicated in the school garden — directly connecting digital and physical inquiry. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/science-subject-kit/build-with-bees

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: History and culture connect the past, present and future.
lo: How technology has changed over time; The purpose and significance of artefacts; How the past connects to the present and future

s.d: Students film themselves 'time travelling' using the green screen — standing in a 1950s kitchen with a record player, then jumping to a modern bedroom with wireless speakers, then leaping to a futuristic room they designed on paper. At each stop they narrate how technology changed, connecting past to present to their prediction about the future of mobile devices. The impossible journey across three eras — made real only by the green screen — turns the unit's technology timeline into a lived, first-person performance. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students produce a 'Then and Now' documentary — filming themselves demonstrating how an old technology artefact from the incursion works (winding up a music box, dialling a rotary phone, loading a cassette) alongside its modern equivalent (playing Spotify, tapping a smartphone, streaming music). Slow-motion and side-by-side editing reveal the transformation, and students narrate the reasons for the change. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students create a short 'Then and Now' video comparing an old technology artefact with its modern equivalent, incorporating the Dance and Drama exploration where they compared dances from different decades and created a timeline of dance that stayed on the classroom wall all term.

Students complete the final activity designing a future mobile device with any features they choose, making a sensible prediction based on the technology evolution traced throughout the unit — from rotary phone to mobile phone to Walkman to Discman to iPhone (Steve Jobs announcement video). They sketch the device, label its features, and record a Seesaw voice note explaining their reasoning. The design prediction is the unit's summative thinking task: applying historical pattern recognition to an imagined future.

Students code an animated timeline in Scratch Jr showing the same technology transforming across generations — a rotary phone ringing, morphing into a mobile phone, changing into a smartphone — with a character pressing each device and triggering a different response. Programming each transformation step by step helps students understand that technology evolves through deliberate human design decisions, not by accident. The act of coding the sequence mirrors the unit's central idea that history connects past, present and future in a chain of cause and effect.

ci: We learn about ourselves through the games we play.
lo: How to identify and manage emotions; How players' choices affect relationships; Our responsibilities when playing games

s.d: Students code a simple game they designed in Scratch Jr — building in the rules, turns and scoring that demonstrate their understanding of fair play and responsibility. When a player breaks a rule, the game shows a consequence on screen (lose a point, go back to start). Comparing different students' games reveals that everyone designed different rules based on what they value — the games ARE reflections of who they are.

Students produce a 'Team Identity Reveal' video — each group films the unveiling of their sports team identity (mascot, colours, body language signals, scoring system, song) created during PE, presenting why each element was chosen and what it expresses about their team's values. Slow-motion captures of the team performing their song and signals create a cinematic reveal moment that celebrates identity and belonging. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students compose their team song in GarageBand — selecting instruments, tempo and lyrics that express their team's personality and values. The song is performed live alongside the team identity reveal video, creating a multi-tool culmination where the GarageBand composition IS the team's anthem, played at sports carnivals and team events throughout the year.

Students build a Lego Spike 'Team Challenge' device where groups must cooperate to program the motor sequence correctly — if one person changes their part without telling the team, the machine fails, demonstrating how teamwork, communication and agreed rules make groups function effectively.

Students photograph their Essential Agreement display and record voice explanations about how the agreed rules help games be fair and fun, connecting to the Kimochi stories about emotions and the Cyber Safety lessons about positive online interactions when playing games.

ci: Artefacts provide a window into the beliefs and values of cultures.
lo: Beliefs and values contribute to a culture; The importance of rituals and traditions; How artefacts capture history and cultural expression

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Cultural Artefact Museum' in Scratch where clicking on artefact sprites triggers animations, recorded voice explanations from their SPO and WWWWH research, and cultural music samples from the Amanecer Andino Bolivian instruments exploration — visitors navigate between museum rooms to discover how artefacts provide windows into the beliefs and values of different cultures, connecting to Kaylea Fern's cultural artefact visit.

Students produce a short cultural documentary featuring their celebration dance (created during Dance expert groups where they researched cultural dances and shared findings with the class), connecting to the 'Why Do People Dance?' YouTube quiz provocation and the exploration of Bolivian music Amanecer Andino.

Students build a Lego Spike 'Artefact Display Turntable' that rotates their physical cultural artefact while the distance sensor detects when a visitor approaches and triggers the motor — presenting their Save the Last Word thinking routine responses alongside the automated display, connecting the Affirmation Beads values activity to an engineering showcase of how technology can preserve and present cultural stories.

Students create a musical response inspired by the cultural music exploration (Amanecer Andino Bolivian instruments, Shenanigans Bush Dances, Bunjil Our Creator), comparing instrument choices and rhythms from different cultures to express cultural stories and traditions.

Students contribute a post to a class 'Artefact Speaks' Padlet — recording a voice note in the voice of one cultural artefact they researched (a didgeridoo, a Māori tā moko, a Chinese jade piece, a Greek amphora), explaining its cultural significance and what beliefs it reveals about the community that created it. Listening to the full Padlet, the class builds a shared understanding of how artefacts from different cultures provide windows into values and beliefs — directly constructing the evidence base for the unit's central idea.

ci: Technology can influence the way ecosystems can survive.
lo: An ecosystem has animals, plants and microorganisms; Technology can cause ecosystems to adapt and evolve; Technology can help or hinder ecosystems

s.d: Students code an interactive ecosystem simulation in Scratch where players add or remove technology (satellites, robots, screens, farming equipment) and watch animated consequences on the animals, plants and microorganisms — connecting to The Wild Robot class novel where different character perspectives revealed technology's complex ethical impacts, and building on the 'Should robots replace teachers?' writing task.

Students produce an iMovie 'Technology on Trial' documentary presenting evidence for and against technology's impact on ecosystems, incorporating footage of their Robot Fact File research, the Screens & Satellites investigation (evolution of TV, before and after the internet), and their developing arguments — using split-screen to show ecosystems with and without technological intervention. This activity works in iMovie, Canva Video, or Adobe Express — use whichever your class prefers. iMovie is the best choice when slow-motion, split-screen, or stop-motion is essential to the task.

Students build a micro:bit 'Ecosystem Monitor' that uses the light and temperature sensors to track environmental conditions in the school garden, displaying a happy plant icon when conditions are good and a warning when they're not — presenting their working device as evidence of how technology can help ecosystems survive, connecting to their summative persuasive writing about ethical dilemmas.

Students create a comic strip in Seesaw (building on the Evolution of TV comic strip activity) showing Before Technology vs After Technology in an ecosystem, using the T-chart and Because/But/So sentence structures practised during the Screens & Satellites investigation.

Students use Google Earth satellite view to explore visible ecosystems from above — coral reefs, rainforests, farms, urban parks — taking screenshots and recording what animals, plants and human-made technology they can spot interacting in each system, building curiosity about how technology can help or hinder ecosystems.

ci: People and the planet are reliant on different energy sources.
lo: Energy types (electricity, fossil fuels, solar); Energy helps us in daily life; Energy transformation; Responsibility to conserve energy and find sustainable sources

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Energy Sources Explorer' in Scratch where clicking on energy source sprites (solar panel, wind turbine, coal plant, battery) triggers animated demonstrations of how each transforms energy, with a dashboard showing sustainability ratings — connecting the bouncing ball, light switch, candle and paper airplane provocation to an interactive simulation visitors can explore and compare.

Students build a Lego Spike motorised circuit demonstration where the motor powers a fan, light or buzzer through a programmable sequence — deliberately breaking the circuit to show what happens when energy flow stops, then presenting their working model alongside their gist statements using the BUT conjunction about fossil fuels vs sustainable energy.

Students film a green screen 'Energy Debate Show' where two teams transport themselves to fossil fuel and renewable energy locations, presenting both sides of the Tug of War provocation ('We need fossil fuels to power our lives') — incorporating their pros and cons reflections and delivering their final gist statement positions as broadcast debaters. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students use micro:bit light sensors to collect data comparing solar energy levels at different times of day, connecting to the solar energy writing exemplar ('Explain how solar energy works and why it is important') and the unit's investigation into sustainable energy alternatives.

Students photograph each step of their circuit building experiment and use drawing tools to label the energy source, wires, switch and bulb, building scientific vocabulary about energy transfer within a closed circuit.

ci: To sustain life, people and environments depend on one another.
lo: Interdependence of people and environments; How people's wants and needs affect the land; Responsibility to use sustainable resources; Connection between fossil fuels and environmental impact

s.d: Students program Beebots to travel a floor map showing the journey from fossil fuel extraction to environmental impact — stopping at each stage (mine/drill, transport, factory, atmosphere, ecosystem) to discuss what happens there and who is affected. The programmed route makes the chain of cause and effect physical and sequential: students must plan every step before the Beebot moves, building a concrete understanding of how human energy choices travel through systems to affect the natural environments and communities that people depend on.

Students code an interactive 'Sustainable Planet' simulation in Scratch where players manage resources by choosing between fossil fuel and renewable energy options — each choice visibly affects the environment sprite (green and healthy vs brown and damaged), connecting their informational writing on fossil fuels to evidence about how Indigenous people look after the land sustainably.

Students film a green screen 'Planet News' broadcast transporting themselves to environments explored during the unit (coral reefs, forests, solar farms, drought-affected areas), delivering informational segments about sustainable vs finite resources — demonstrating vocabulary built across both HTWW and STP units while connecting to their inquiry about whether everyday devices could be charged by the sun. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students create a 'Sounds of the Seasons' composition connecting the Star Wars and Star Trek film music rhythm pattern activities to their Chinese language learning about seasons, exploring how music communicates ideas about nature and the planet across cultures.

Students use Google Earth to explore environments connected to energy and sustainability — navigating to coal mines in the Hunter Valley, offshore wind farms in Denmark, solar arrays in the Mojave Desert, and areas of deforestation in Brazil. They compare satellite imagery of energy landscapes under different management regimes, gathering visual evidence for the unit's inquiry into how people's choices about resources affect the environments and communities that depend on them. The aerial perspective makes the abstract concept of interdependence concrete and visible.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Journeys can lead to challenges and new understandings.
lo: Types of journeys (physical, learning, cultural); Challenges encountered on journeys; New understandings gained through journeys

s.d: Students produce an Canva Video 'Family Journey Documentary' combining their home learning interview footage (using WWWWH question syntax), family photos, the wave template showing challenges, opportunities and growth, and connections to picture storybooks like 'I Am Sangoel' and 'The Snail and the Whale' — narrating their family's significant journey with titles, transitions and a soundtrack.

Students film a green screen broadcast where they 'travel' to the locations from their family's journey story, reporting from each place about the challenges encountered and growth that resulted — transporting themselves into the settings of picture storybooks (Little Chelone's ocean, Sangoel's Sudan) to make physical, learning and cultural journeys visible. The green screen backdrop effect can also be achieved using Canva Video's built-in chroma key feature if a physical green screen setup isn't available — no special hardware required.

Students code an animated journey map in Scratch Jr where a character sprite travels between location backdrops from the picture storybooks explored in the unit (The Snail & the Whale, Little Chelone, I Am Sangoel, Alfred's War), with recorded voice narrating what challenges and growth happened at each stop — connecting CSPACE summary skills to interactive storytelling.

Students explore the actual locations from the picture storybooks using Google Earth — sailing the Atlantic from The Snail and the Whale, locating Sudan from I Am Sangoel, tracing the Pacific from Little Chelone, finding Australia from Alfred's War. Navigating between locations on the satellite globe helps students visualise the true scale of physical journeys and understand why migration challenges are real and significant. The geographic grounding builds empathy before students research their own family's significant journey.

Students complete the Challenges, Opportunities and Growth reflection comparing their own learning journey through the unit to the family journey they researched, using the wave template format to show how journeys are not always smooth.

ci: The way we interact with others is influenced by who we are
lo: Personal identity and strengths; How self-regulation supports learning; Building resilience through knowledge of ourselves

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Calm Palm Toolkit' in Scratch Jr where tapping different strategy sprites (deep breathing, counting, walking away, talking to someone) triggers animated demonstrations of each self-regulation technique from their Calm Palm template — creating a digital wellbeing resource they can share with younger students.

Students produce an Canva Video 'Letter to my Year 3 Teacher' video combining their All About Me Selfie, self-portrait, Frames of Strength reflections and Mind Monsters drawings with narration about who they are, their strengths, and how UR STRONG Friendship Fires and Growth Mindset lessons built their resilience — a personalised documentary impossible without film production tools.

Students compose a personal 'Strengths Soundtrack' in GarageBand where each instrument layer represents a different strength from their Frames of Strength activity — combining the tracks into a composition that expresses their identity through sound, then presenting why they chose each instrument to represent each strength and how the Essential Agreement connects them.

Students create a 'Strengths and Strategies' page in Book Creator — drawing a self-portrait, writing three personal strengths they've identified through the unit's activities (the All About Me Self-Portrait, the BOB strategy exploration, the Calm Palm toolkit), and recording a voice note explaining which self-regulation strategy they found most useful and why. The multimodal self-portrait becomes a reflective artefact capturing who the student is at this point in their learning, directly connecting to the unit's central idea that how we interact with others is influenced by who we are.

Students photograph their Calm Palm template and Mind Monsters drawings, recording voice explanations about which strategies help them self-regulate in different situations — building their 'ready to learn' toolkit throughout the year.

ci: Methods of communication have different features which impact their effectiveness
lo: The forms of verbal, non-verbal and visual communication; How the correct form enhances the effectiveness of a message; How people interpret messages and bring their own meaning to interactions

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Communication Through Time' game in Scratch where players must decode messages sent using different methods (smoke signals, morse code, telephone, text, emoji) — each level introduces a new form of communication and players discover how the correct form enhances the effectiveness of a message, connecting to their GarageBand dynamics exploration.

Students produce their silent movie (planned, rehearsed and filmed with their group — choosing location, characters and costumes), demonstrating how non-verbal communication conveys meaning without words, then use the class-created success criteria for self and peer assessment of the final product — inspired by the Mr Bean clips (At the Dentist, The Hairdresser) that provoked discussion about body language and facial expressions.

Students create a ‘Communication Through Time’ video exploring how modes of communication have developed to meet specific needs, featuring their research into Morse code, Braille and Auslan from Library sessions, the Ancient Egyptian hieroglyphics explored in Visual Art, and music signs and symbols (dynamics, tempo, legato/staccato) that composers use to direct performances.

Students build a micro:bit 'Communication Signal Generator' — programming it to send coded messages (Morse code patterns using the LED matrix, or light flashes) to a partner's micro:bit using the radio feature. Partners decode the message without speaking, then discuss: how is this different from speaking? From writing? From a phone call? The hands-on experience of encoding and decoding a message builds the unit's central idea that different communication methods have different features — and that the method shapes how effectively a message is received.

Students compose a short piece using dynamics and articulation (legato/staccato) to communicate different emotions without words, connecting to the music focus on how composers use signs, symbols, tempo and dynamics to shape expression — and the role of the conductor in interpreting and communicating musical ideas.

ci: Ethical decision making supports online choices.
lo: How choice of action and reaction affects others online; How informed choices can make a positive difference; Our right to feel safe online and the responsibility to make ethical decisions

s.d: Students create an anonymous 'dataselfie' on a class Padlet — posting 5 things they've searched online, 4 things they've liked, 3 videos they've watched, 2 places they've visited with a phone, and 1 person they've messaged. With names hidden, the class then tries to identify who each post belongs to based solely on the data. The moment students realise they can be identified from their digital habits — with no name attached — is the unit's most powerful ethical provocation: every online action leaves a trace, and informed choices about what we share directly affect our right to feel safe online.

Students code an interactive choose-your-own-adventure about online safety where choices lead to different ethical outcomes, building on the Cyber Safety Project lessons and the Timon and Pumbaa Safety Smart video — demonstrating understanding that things posted online are there forever and that informed choices can make a positive difference.

Students produce a cyber safety awareness video demonstrating responsible online behaviour, using scenarios from the Cyber Safety Project lessons and real examples of ethical choices — connecting to the traffic light self-assessment where students evaluated how well they follow the Essential Agreement, and the student-initiated action of teaching siblings about being safe online.

Students design a digital citizenship guide showing the Online Essential Agreement they collaboratively created, with scenarios demonstrating ethical decision-making for different online situations — incorporating perspectives from the homework task where families discussed online usage and the class categorisation of apps/websites into communication, information and recreation.

Students create an online safety quiz testing peers on ethical digital scenarios, incorporating questions about when to alert a teacher (like the student who reported a pop-up at home), how to evaluate trustworthy sources, and the difference between responsible and irresponsible online choices — used as a review tool connecting to the Principled Learner Profile focus.

ci: Materials behave and interact in certain ways, which poses challenges and solutions
lo: The three states of matter and their properties; How matter changes states depending on particles, temperature and energy; Responsible and ethical use of materials like glass and plastic

s.d: Students conduct a class rubbish audit using Microsoft Forms to record waste by category — plastic, glass, paper, organic, metal — the exact materials the unit asks them to evaluate for responsible and ethical use. Forms auto-generates bar charts from the tally data instantly, making it visible which materials are most wasteful without needing manual graphing. Students compare findings across classes on Padlet, then connect the data to the unit's inquiry into responsible material use — proposing one realistic change the school could make. The audit turns an abstract ethical question into a school-specific, evidence-based argument.

Students produce an iMovie 'Materials Lab' episode where they film their best states of matter experiment with slow-motion capture of the state change moment, overlay particle behaviour animations from their Scratch simulation, and deliver scientific explanations using vocabulary from their Freeform thinking boards — creating a documentary that makes invisible particle behaviour visible.

Students build a Lego Spike 'State Change Detector' that uses the temperature sensor to monitor their ice cube melting experiment in real time, programming the motor to trigger a flag when the melting point is reached — presenting their automated experiment alongside measured data showing how adding variables (salt, sugar, sand) affected melting rates.

Students design a 3D model in Tinkercad of their chocolate-making flow chart as a physical production line with stations for each state change (solid cocoa → melted liquid → moulded solid), showing how responsible and ethical use of materials connects to understanding reversible and irreversible changes — a design that could be 3D printed as a teaching model.

Students use micro:bit temperature sensors to collect data during the ice cube melting experiment — tracking how quickly ice melts on different material surfaces (glass, metal, wood, plastic) and recording readings at one-minute intervals. The digital data makes the difference between materials' thermal conductivity measurable and comparable rather than just observable. Results across different groups are compiled into a class dataset, revealing patterns in how material properties affect heat transfer — connecting the hands-on experiment to the unit's inquiry into responsible use of glass and plastic.

ci: People can respond to natural disasters with resilience
lo: The causes and types of natural disasters (tectonic events, weather events); How natural disasters impact the environment and people; How humans respond with resilience and connection

s.d: Students produce a news-style documentary report about their researched natural disaster using green screen to ‘report live’ from the location, incorporating behind-the-news style presentation of tectonic plates, the science of tsunamis, and how bushfire management connects to Indigenous cool burns vs European practices — referencing the BTN episodes and the poem ‘My Country’ by Dorothea Mackellar they learned.

Students use micro:bit accelerometers to create a simple earthquake detection model, programming it to display an alert when shaking is detected above a threshold — connecting to their research about how Japan measures earthquakes and uses early warning systems, and the class discussion about whether natural disasters can be prevented.

Students design a natural disaster newspaper-style report about a well-known event they researched (Cyclone Tracy, Boxing Day Tsunami, Christchurch earthquake), incorporating the SPO note-taking structure, their classification of disasters into tectonic events (earthquakes, volcanoes, tsunamis) and weather events (cyclones, tornadoes, floods, bushfires) — and how communities respond with resilience, drawing on the Japan case study about earthquake preparation.

Students create a ‘Natural Disaster Survival Guide’ featuring their earthquake backpack sorting activity, the Japan earthquake preparation research (from Japanology videos and the PowerPoint), flood fact files, and their design comprehension work on building for disaster-prone environments — connecting to the student-initiated action of incorporating disaster-resistant features when designing buildings in the WWAIPAT city unit.

Students document their volcano investigation experiment and natural disaster science experiments with step-by-step photos and annotations, recording their self-assessment reflections (‘What have I done well? What am I proud of? What could I improve?’) and sharing peer feedback on their newspaper articles and disaster-resistant building designs.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Many factors shape the development of cities.
lo: The essential features of a city; The function of a city; The development of cities through time

s.d: Students build a 3D virtual version of the class city they collaboratively created, coding interactive elements showing where buildings were strategically placed (hospital, school, green spaces) and incorporating natural disaster-resistant features learned from the STP unit — with labels in Chinese for city facilities and transport as explored in the Chinese Travel Project.

Students create a city soundscape inspired by Mussorgsky’s ‘Pictures at an Exhibition’, selecting instruments to represent different parts of their class city (traffic, markets, parks, construction) and connecting to the music listening activities where they described how composers are inspired by their surroundings to create works representing city scenes.

Students design a city comparison presentation featuring their Venn Diagram analysis of Mexico City vs Paris, research from the Mega Cities YouTube videos about Tokyo and New York, and insights from Mr Rory Hyde’s incursion (architect and University of Melbourne lecturer) about urban planning in Shepparton and flood-prone design — explaining how demographic, density and social factors shape city development.

Students rebuild the class's collaborative city in Minecraft Education — translating their hand-drawn plans, clay models, and discussions with Mr Rory Hyde into a navigable 3D environment. Each city zone (residential, commercial, civic, green space, transport) must be placed according to the urban planning principles explored during the unit: schools near homes, parks distributed equitably, transport connecting all zones. Students then introduce a natural disaster challenge (flood zone, earthquake fault) researched during the Sharing the Planet unit and adapt their city design accordingly — adding elevated structures, flood barriers, or reinforced buildings. The Minecraft city becomes a living design document that records the class's evolving understanding of what makes a city function.

Students use Google Earth to explore the cities they researched during the unit — Tokyo, New York, Paris, Mexico City — navigating from satellite view down to street level to identify essential features: transport hubs, civic buildings, parks, commercial districts, and residential areas. They compare the spatial organisation of different cities, identifying similarities that reveal universal urban functions and differences that reflect local culture and history. The virtual exploration grounds the unit's inquiry into what makes a city, what functions it serves, and how cities develop over time.

Students explore the 'World Cities and Civilizations' collection — travelling through history to discover how cities developed in response to geography, trade, governance and human need. They compare the historical city models with their own collaborative class city design, identifying which planning decisions from history appear in their build and which they chose differently. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/social-studies/World_Cities_and_Civilizations

ci: Health and wellbeing are impacted by the choices we make
lo: How our body systems connect and function; How our choices affect physical, emotional and mental wellbeing; Strategies to measure and maintain the health of the human body

s.d: Students build an interactive 3D 'Body Systems Explorer' in CoSpaces where visitors walk through a virtual human body, clicking on organs within their chosen body system (digestive, respiratory, circulatory, muscular, skeletal) to trigger explanations and animations showing how the system functions — presenting their virtual tour at a class health expo.

Students build a micro:bit 'Fitness Dashboard' that uses the accelerometer to track physical activity and displays real-time data on the LED screen, programming custom icons for different fitness components — presenting their device alongside their PE fitness testing results to demonstrate how choices about exercise affect health and wellbeing.

Students produce a ‘Healthy Choices’ podcast episode discussing how choices affect physical and mental wellbeing, drawing on their PE fitness goal-setting (Term 1 baseline vs Term 4 retest to measure improvement), the Yoga Spin Game and Yoga Dice activities, and the Dance and Drama connection between movement and mental health — referencing the warm-up and cool-down routines students created and ran for the class. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students collaboratively create a Kahoot quiz testing their peers on body systems knowledge — writing questions based on their chosen system research (digestive, respiratory, circulatory, muscular, skeletal) and connecting to PE fitness testing results and the components of fitness explored during the unit.

Students use Freeform to build a class 'Body Systems Connection Map' — each group adding their assigned body system (digestive, circulatory, respiratory, nervous) and then drawing connections to every other system it interacts with. The completed board reveals that no system works in isolation: every choice students make (food, exercise, sleep, stress) ripples across multiple systems simultaneously. The map becomes the visual backbone for the unit's argument that health and wellbeing are interconnected, not separate.

Students use the Minecraft Education SEL world collection — which includes scenarios focused on emotional wellbeing, empathy and responsible decision-making — as a discussion provocation for the unit's inquiry into how health choices affect wellbeing. They play through a scenario together, identify the health choices being made, and discuss what the character could do differently — making abstract health concepts concrete and discussable. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/social-studies/Social_Emotional_Learning

ci: The creative process involves understanding and applying artistic elements to communicate ideas and emotions across disciplines
lo: How colour, movement and music communicate character and mood; The creative process of making a multi-disciplinary presentation; The effort and commitment required to produce a live performance

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Beijing Opera Performance Guide' in Scratch where visitors click on character type sprites (clown, warrior, villain, scholar) to trigger animations showing how colour, movement and costume communicate character and mood — connecting their GarageBand musical compositions, mask designs and partnership vignette performances into one interactive experience.

Students film and edit their Beijing Opera partnership vignette performance featuring their chosen character (clown, warrior, villain or female character), incorporating their hand-painted character mask, scripted backstory, exaggerated mime movements and body acting, and musical ensemble accompaniment, then adding a narrated reflection explaining how their colour choices express their character's personality traits as studied in Chinese class.

Students design a comparative presentation exploring the meaning of colours across different cultures studied in Chinese class, connecting their findings to the colour choices made for their Beijing Opera mask, and showing how the same colour can express different character traits and personalities depending on cultural context, drawing on the See-Think-Wonder observations about why Beijing Opera masks are so exaggerated.

Students compose and record their musical ensemble piece for the Beijing Opera performance in partnership groups, experimenting with tempo and dynamics to create mood and introduce their character, building on Year 3 skills of using tempo and dynamics to create drama in music, and selecting instruments that match the tradition of Chinese opera as explored during the Find Out phase.

Students choose a character in Adobe Express Character Animator inspired by the Beijing Opera unit — selecting bold colours that communicate the character's role (red for loyalty, white for treachery, black for integrity) and recording their voice performing a short monologue in character. The animation syncs to their voice automatically; they add music and a stage background. The finished piece demonstrates the unit's central idea directly: that colour, movement and music work together to communicate character and mood, with every artistic choice deliberate and justifiable.

ci: Not-for-profit organisations exist to respond to a variety of needs within our community
lo: The function and purpose of not-for-profit organisations; How not-for-profit organisations respond to community needs; Our responsibility to take action and make a difference

s.d: Students code an interactive 'NFP Impact Explorer' in Scratch where visitors click on organisation sprites to see animated demonstrations of how each not-for-profit responds to community needs — featuring data from their partner research into two NFP organisations and connecting to their group's community action journey.

Students create a short documentary showcasing their group's community action journey from identifying the big issue through to organising their contribution drive, including interviews with group members explaining why they chose their particular action, footage of their Junior School classroom visits promoting the different actions, and their Great Space display where contributions were collected, celebrating that the cohort raised over $1,500 plus 200 cans and 250 books.

Students design promotional flyers and posters for their chosen community action (OzHarvest 50-cent meal donations, St Kilda Mums collection, Lions Club book drive, ARDOCH tinned food drive, or artwork sales with Prep students), incorporating the persuasive action slogans developed in class, formatted to contribute to the flyer quilt of actions displayed and distributed across the Junior School.

Students create a peer and self-assessment survey for their collaborative group work during the action planning phase, rating teamwork skills after each session and formulating individual and group goals for the next session as referenced in the planner's ongoing assessment, supporting growth in collaboration and self-management skills throughout the fundraising and collection drive experience.

Students use Minecraft Education to build a Minecraft representation of their chosen not-for-profit organisation's impact — a food relief distribution centre (OzHarvest), a children's resource hub (St Kilda Mums), a community library (Lions Club book drive). The build must include realistic functional zones based on research into how the organisation actually operates: intake area, sorting, storage, distribution, client spaces. Students annotate each zone with signs explaining what happens there, who it serves, and what would happen to that community need if the organisation didn't exist. The build transforms abstract research into a spatial argument for why NFPs matter, and the class tour of each other's builds creates a collective understanding of the diverse needs NFPs respond to across the community.

ci: Engineers design and build structures using specific features and materials to make them strong, stable and fit for purpose
lo: Different types of bridges and their design features; How materials and engineering principles contribute to structural strength and stability; Testing and refining designs through experimentation

s.d: Students program a CoDrone EDU to perform a controlled flyover inspection of all bridge designs displayed around the classroom, capturing aerial footage that reveals structural features from above — arch shapes, truss triangulation, tower positioning. They then present the drone footage alongside micro:bit load-testing data, explaining how beam, truss and suspension designs distribute forces differently. The aerial perspective combined with real-time digital measurement creates a multi-modal engineering analysis that goes beyond what physical inspection alone can show.

Students create a 3D digital model of their bridge design, applying knowledge of beam, truss and suspension bridge features learned during the Seesaw research task, demonstrating how appropriate design features and materials ensure structural stability, and comparing their refined digital design with the physical bridge they constructed with their partner during the Building Bridge Challenge.

Students build a motorised Lego Spike drawbridge or lifting mechanism as an extension of the Building Bridge Challenge — programming the motor to raise and lower at specified intervals while testing structural integrity under load. The challenge introduces variables the physical bridge didn't have: timing, motor power, mechanical advantage. This prompts students to revisit how engineering features affect both stability and function, connecting directly to the Tacoma Bridge Collapse analysis where unanticipated variables caused catastrophic failure.

Students program a Micro:bit sensor to measure and digitally display the load placed on their bridge during testing, replacing the manual coin-counting method with digital data collection that records each addition, and comparing results across different bridge designs to identify which engineering features (beam, truss, suspension) contributed most to stability and load-bearing capacity.

Students use Minecraft Education to design and test their bridge structure before building it physically — constructing a digital prototype of their beam, truss, or suspension bridge and stress-testing it by placing progressively heavier loads (represented by weighted blocks) at the midpoint and observing where structural failure begins. The Minecraft build forces students to think through engineering principles spatially: triangulation in a truss must actually triangulate, arch supports must curve correctly, tension cables must connect at the right anchor points. Comparing the digital build's failure points with their physical bridge's coin-test performance reveals which engineering features translate from design to reality and which don't — directly connecting to the Tacoma Bridge Collapse analysis of how unanticipated variables cause failure.

ci: First Nations people have a deep connection to Country, language and culture
lo: The diversity of language and culture across First Nations communities in Australia; The significance of connection to Country as custodial responsibility; Listening to and learning from Indigenous perspectives

s.d: Students build an interactive 3D 'Connection to Country' virtual experience in CoSpaces where visitors walk through landscapes representing the indigenous countries they researched (such as the Arrernte and Boon Wurrung), clicking on elements to hear recorded explanations about connection to Country, language diversity, and cultural significance — incorporating their acrylic canvas painting and artist's statement.

Students create a documentary about their understanding of First Nations connection to Country, incorporating footage and reflections from the Melbourne Museum First Nations exhibition excursion, their soundscape performances representing pages from 'Australia from Dawn to Dusk' by Brentos, and narration explaining the concept of Country as custodial responsibility rather than ownership, as defined during the Tune In phase.

DLP captures aerial footage of the school grounds and local environment before and after the Melbourne Museum excursion, providing a bird's-eye perspective of Country that students compare with the Khatija Possum canvas painting style and the 'Australia from Dawn to Dusk' soundscape imagery studied during the unit.

Students use Seesaw to compile their Venn diagram research comparing three different First Nations communities — recording voice annotations explaining the connections and distinctions they found between communities' relationships to Country, language, and kinship structures. The voice recording layer is critical: students must articulate in their own words why the similarities and differences matter, moving beyond surface comparison to genuine understanding. The completed Seesaw entries become the formative assessment evidence for the unit's inquiry into the diversity of First Nations peoples across Australia.

Students collaborate in groups to create their soundscape representing a page from 'Australia from Dawn to Dusk', experimenting with digital instruments and recorded sounds to evoke a specific time of day, Australian animals, scenery and location, building on the musical decision-making practised during Sort Out where groups chose how to represent the landscape and atmosphere of their assigned page.

Students explore this world collection — created in partnership with First Nations communities — to experience how diverse Indigenous peoples express connection to Country, language and culture. They compare what they encounter with knowledge from the Melbourne Museum First Nations exhibition and the 'Australia from Dawn to Dusk' soundscape work, critically discussing what digital representation can and cannot convey about living culture. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/social-studies/Indigenous_Cultures_and_History

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration and discovery have led to changes that shape the world we live in
lo: The age of exploration and the reasons explorers set out on their journeys; How Australia changed through European arrival; Different perspectives on the consequences of exploration

s.d: Students program a CoDrone EDU to navigate a floor map tracing the actual Age of Exploration routes — flying from Spain toward the Americas, rounding the Cape of Good Hope, or crossing the Pacific. At each waypoint they must recalculate and reprogram, experiencing firsthand why explorers had to plan meticulously, adapt to the unexpected, and navigate with incomplete information. The drone mission connects coding to the unit's inquiry into the reasons for exploration and the consequences of reaching new lands — including whose lands they were.

Students plot the journeys of the explorers they researched using the laminated cards, tracing routes from Europe to Australia and other destinations during the Age of Exploration (14th–16th century), adding location pins with key facts from their Seesaw research task, and comparing the historical maps they ordered from oldest to newest in the Tune In activity with modern satellite imagery of the same regions.

Students create an interactive timeline animation showing the Age of Exploration, programming explorer sprites to move along their routes with pop-up information cards displaying key facts gathered from the Inquisitive video and laminated card research, helping them visualise the sequence of discoveries and the connections between different explorers' journeys.

Students build a Minecraft Education recreation of a key location from the Age of Exploration they studied — the deck of a ship on a long Pacific voyage, a colonial contact site, or the interior of Australia as Burke and Wills encountered it. Every spatial decision is anchored in the explorer research: students must place resources, hazards, and landmarks where the historical record puts them, and annotate each zone with signs explaining what happened there and from whose perspective. The dual-perspective framing from the unit is embedded directly into the build — students must include both the coloniser's viewpoint and the perspective of those already living there, making the 'different perspectives on the consequences of exploration' line of inquiry physically present in the constructed world.

Students choose a character in Adobe Express Character Animator to represent a historical figure from the Age of Exploration — an explorer, an Indigenous Australian at the time of first contact, or a settler — and record their first-person account. The character animates to their voice with a historically appropriate background, and students deliberately choose whose perspective to voice, then compare how the same events look completely different depending on the character chosen. The unit's key LO — different perspectives on the consequences of exploration — comes alive when students hear the same event voiced by opposite sides.

ci: Leaders can help a group to function effectively
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; Purpose of leadership; The strengths and qualities of a leader

s.d: Students produce a podcast profile of their chosen leader from the 'Little People, Big Dreams' research — structured as a three-part critical analysis: the leadership quality that defines this person, the specific community challenge they led their way through, and one decision they made that a less effective leader would not have. The third question is the hardest and most valuable — it requires students to understand not just what their leader did, but why that decision was difficult and what it demanded of them. When the class listens to each other's profiles, they identify which leadership qualities recur across different people, places, and time periods, collectively building the unit's framework for what effective leadership actually looks like in practice. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students build an interactive 3D 'Leadership Showcase' in CoSpaces where visitors walk through exhibit rooms for each leader from the 'Little People, Big Dreams' research, clicking on objects to trigger recorded presentations about leadership qualities, purpose, and impact — creating a virtual exhibition that brings their independent research to life.

Students design a leadership attributes presentation showcasing the qualities identified through the collaborative brainstorm and Lucky Dip popsicle activity, explaining why each attribute is important for effective leadership with real examples from the Cross Campus Leadership Day, the Anthony Albanese provocation discussion, and their own experience fulfilling their portfolio role throughout the year.

Students use Freeform to map how their Year 4 student leadership council connects to the real leadership structures studied during the unit — school captains, community leaders, historical figures. They identify which leadership qualities appear in their own council, which responsibilities match, and where the gaps are. The collaborative map becomes a mirror: students see their own leadership experience reflected against the unit's inquiry into roles, responsibilities and the purpose of leadership in communities.

Students design and conduct a class survey in Microsoft Forms gathering perspectives on roles and responsibilities in leadership — asking classmates what qualities they value most in a leader, what responsibilities they think student leaders should have, and whether they feel represented by their school's leadership structures. After collecting and analysing responses, each student compares the class data with their research on historical and community leaders, identifying where their peers' expectations match and diverge from what effective leadership actually requires. The survey design process forces students to operationalise the unit's leadership concepts as measurable questions.

ci: Social norms and stereotypes can influence the way people express themselves
lo: How stereotypes and social norms form and persist in society; The impact of discrimination, prejudice and assumptions on individuals and communities; How people overcome stereotypes to express themselves and inspire change

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Breaking Stereotypes' choose-your-own-adventure in Scratch where players encounter stereotype scenarios and must decide how to respond — each choice triggers animated consequences showing how challenging social norms creates positive change, connecting their rights activist research to an experience that puts players in the shoes of someone facing discrimination.

Students create a short awareness video inspired by the student-initiated posters and slogans displayed in the classroom, presenting scenarios from the Stereotypes Scenarios activity and demonstrating how discrimination, prejudice and assumptions can be forms of disrespect, drawing on what they learned from the guest manga illustrator speaker about how art can convey powerful messages that challenge social norms.

Students compose original soundtracks or jingles in GarageBand to accompany their awareness posters and slogans for the student action campaign against stereotypes, selecting instruments and moods that reinforce their message, inspired by the media techniques analysed during the Graffiti Article discussion and Famous People Infographics research.

Students design their Famous People Infographic using the PowerPoint template structure, presenting research into a person who overcame a stereotype, prejudice, discrimination or social norm and paved the way for others, including key facts about what they faced, how they expressed themselves despite barriers, and their lasting impact on society, ready for sharing in mixed small groups during the peer feedback sessions.

Students use Freeform to collaboratively map how stereotypes and social norms form and persist in society, connecting examples from their Gallery Walk See-Think-Wonder provocation, rights activist research, and student action campaign planning — building a visual analysis of how discrimination, prejudice and assumptions interconnect.

ci: Goods and services are exchanged within a marketplace
lo: The production of goods from raw materials through manufacturing to consumer; The exchange of currency for goods and services in a functioning economy; Ethics and responsible decision-making in the marketplace

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Suburb Simulation' economics game in Scratch where players manage a business within a virtual marketplace — buying raw materials, manufacturing products, setting prices and serving customers — with supply and demand mechanics that dynamically change prices, connecting to their real suburb simulation experience and demonstrating how ethics and responsible decision-making affect marketplace success.

Students manage their suburb simulation personal budget using the pre-coded spreadsheet with formulas, tracking their startup bonus, role salary payments, fines, rent, utilities and business income/expenses, performing scheduled and surprise reconciliations of receipts against their spreadsheet entries, and analysing profit and loss to make informed decisions about pricing, supply and demand in their student-run businesses.

Students design professional business proposal presentations for their student-created businesses within the suburb simulation, including their business name and logo, the goods or services offered, pricing strategy informed by supply and demand principles learned through Sam's Soccer Ball Factory activity, and their ethical business practices, ready for presentation at city meetings where peers evaluate and provide feedback.

Students design customer satisfaction surveys for their student-run businesses within the suburb simulation, gathering peer feedback on product quality, pricing fairness and ethical practices, then analysing the response data during city and suburb meetings to make evidence-based decisions about improving their goods and services and adjusting their business strategies for the next trading session.

Students complete the unit evaluation reflection task documenting what they learned about how supply and demand drive an economy, how ethics affect business success (referencing specific examples from their suburb trading experiences such as adjusting prices based on customer demand), and how managing a real budget with spreadsheets helped them understand the connection between consumer behaviour and the marketplace.

ci: Inventions and initiatives are a response to the changing needs of society
lo: How inventions have changed over time to meet the demands of society; The UN Sustainable Development Goals and the global problems they address; Using the design thinking process to create innovations that respond to real-world needs

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Changed Over Time' museum in Scratch where visitors click on invention category sprites (household, transport, communication, medicine) to see animated timelines showing how each invention evolved to meet changing societal demands — connecting their decades research T-Chart data to an interactive exhibition visitors can explore and compare across eras.

Students produce a Shark Tank-style invention pitch video presenting their group's SDG-connected invention to a panel, explaining which Sustainable Development Goal it addresses, how they followed the d:school design process (Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test), what need in society it responds to, and how it works, incorporating feedback received from peers and families at the Future Thinkers' Forum stalls.

Students build motorised invention prototypes using Lego Spike during the Light Boxes and STEAM lab sessions with Stefan, applying the d:school design process (empathise, define, ideate, prototype, test) to create working models that address an SDG challenge identified during the SDG ordering, grouping, and T-chart activities.

Students explore drone technology as a modern invention during the Changed Over Time decades research, programming CoDrone EDU flight paths to demonstrate how innovations build on each other as explored in 'Everything is a Remix', and investigating how drones connect to specific SDGs such as sustainable agriculture or disaster response.

Students program micro:bit sensors to collect environmental data (temperature, light, sound) as part of their invention prototypes during the STEAM lab sessions with Stefan, connecting sensor technology to SDG solutions and presenting their data at the Future Thinkers' Forum alongside their design process booklets.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for conservation and environmental stewardship; Ways in which First Nations land management practices can drive positive environmental change

s.d: Students build a working soil moisture sensor for the WesMob kitchen garden or a classroom pot plant — programming a BBC micro:bit with two nails and crocodile clips to read soil conductivity and display a moisture level on the LED matrix. They set a threshold below which the micro:bit triggers an alert, warning the class the garden needs water. Students then investigate different planting zones around the school, collecting and comparing data to propose a sustainable watering schedule. The sensor makes the invisible visible — real conductivity data from real soil — connecting directly to the unit's inquiry into how community values shape environmental stewardship, and reflecting First Nations land management principles of caring for Country through close, attentive observation.

Students code an interactive 'Biome Explorer' in Scratch where visitors click on world biome sprites to see animated ecosystems showing how community values shape environmental management — incorporating satellite imagery comparisons from Google Earth, First Nations land management practices from the WesMob garden, and conservation data from the Chum Creek and ECOSS learning experiences.

Students create a digital campaign page inspired by the Melbourne Urban Forest initiative, featuring the emails they wrote to trees around Wesley College during the Go Further phase, information about how city stewardship programs support environmental change, photographs from their ECOSS visit documenting the veggie garden, bee farm, worm farms and chickens, and their proposals for continued native planting around the school with the Environment Prefects.

DLP captures aerial footage of the WesMob native garden and the Chum Creek camp surroundings, providing a bird's-eye perspective of the habitats and conservation landscapes students study during the Indigenous Land Management lessons and the ECOSS visit, giving context for their stewardship vocabulary work.

Students use Minecraft Education to build two versions of the same ecosystem — the WesMob kitchen garden, a local wetland, or a habitat studied during the Habitats of the World research — one representing healthy, well-managed conditions and one showing the impact of land clearing, introduced species, or water contamination. Every structural difference between the two worlds must be justified by evidence: the healthy version has the plant species, water features, and shelter structures that support the target animal; the degraded version removes or replaces them based on the actual threats their conservation research identified. Students tour each other's paired worlds and identify the stewardship interventions needed to restore the damaged ecosystem — translating research into spatial, actionable recommendations.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has had a lasting impact on different countries and their people
lo: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; How colonisation transformed society and culture; The responsibility of colonisers and the perspectives of those colonised

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Perspectives on Colonisation' simulation in Scratch where visitors click on different character sprites (coloniser, indigenous elder, settler, modern historian) to hear recorded perspectives on the same event — demonstrating how colonisation is understood differently depending on point of view, connecting to their note-taking and summarising research.

Students produce a podcast episode presenting colonisation through two voices — one episode, two recorded perspectives on the same historical event. One student voices a coloniser explaining their motivations; a second voices an Indigenous Australian or colonised person responding to the direct consequences of those same actions. Scripts are grounded in evidence from class research, ClickView resources, and the Convict Boy novel. Playing the paired recordings back-to-back demonstrates the unit's central idea directly: colonisation didn't just transform society — it transformed it completely differently depending on whose land, culture, and family bore the cost. The class collection of paired episodes becomes a multi-perspective archive of colonisation's lasting impact. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students program a CoDrone EDU to navigate a floor map tracing colonisation routes — from Britain to Australia, from Europe to Africa, from Spain to the Americas. At each waypoint they encounter a decision card: how did the colonisers treat the people already living here? What changed? Students reprogram the drone based on their decision, experiencing how colonisation was not a single inevitable event but a series of choices with lasting consequences — connecting the physical route to the unit's central idea about lasting impact.

Students use Pico VR to explore historical sites and environments connected to British colonisation, virtually visiting locations referenced during the ClickView Terra Nullius viewing and the Convict Boy class novel, building contextual understanding before completing their British colonisation mini SAT and oral presentations with peer assessment.

Students choose a character in Adobe Express Character Animator to voice a perspective on colonisation — an Indigenous Australian at the time of first contact, a coloniser, or a present-day descendant reflecting on lasting impact. Recording their first-person account with a historically grounded background, the animation syncs to their voice automatically. The class collection of animated perspectives — voiced by the same students, but from opposite sides — becomes a powerful demonstration of the unit's central idea: that colonisation transformed society and culture differently depending on whose story is being told.

Students explore this world collection to experience the stories and cultural practices of First Nations peoples — including the lasting impacts of colonisation on communities, language and Country. They compare what the Minecraft worlds represent with the primary sources, the 'Unsettled' resource, and the carved spotted gum investigation from class, critically discussing what each medium can and cannot convey about the ongoing effects of colonisation. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/social-studies/Indigenous_Cultures_and_History

ci: Values, beliefs and actions contribute to a diverse society
lo: How our values and beliefs shape our identity and interactions; Our responsibility to manage emotions and be safe online and in person; The importance of resilience, respect and positive relationships in a diverse community

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Digital Citizenship Simulator' in Scratch where players navigate online scenarios from the 16 CyberSafety lessons and must make ethical choices — each decision triggers animated consequences showing how values, beliefs and responsible digital actions contribute to a diverse and safe society, connecting to their URStrong and Grow Your Mind program learning.

Students produce a podcast episode examining a real ethical dilemma from the 16 CyberSafety lessons — a situation where values, beliefs, and digital actions collide. Each episode presents the dilemma, explores it from at least two perspectives (including one the student personally disagrees with), and argues for a specific course of action with clear reasoning. The podcast format requires students to hold their position under analytical scrutiny — they must explain their values, not just assert them. A second student then records a two-minute response episode, agreeing or disagreeing with the argument and explaining why. The paired format creates genuine intellectual dialogue about digital citizenship rather than one-directional presentation. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students design a 'CyberSafety Golden Rule' infographic presenting the personal online safety rule they created during the Cyber Safety Project, incorporating strategies learned across the 16 CyberSafety lessons about responsible technology use, being an upstander, recognising online scams, and protecting personal information, with visual examples of how to apply their rule in real scenarios.

Students create interactive quizzes for their peers based on key concepts from the 16 CyberSafety lessons and 11 URStrong sessions, testing classmates' knowledge of online safety strategies, friendship fire resolution techniques, upstander behaviours, and the difference between values, beliefs and actions, reinforcing learning through peer-teaching during the Find Out phase.

Students use Freeform to collaboratively map how stereotypes and social norms they've encountered — in media, at school, in the Famous People research — form, persist, and can be challenged. Each student adds branches connecting a stereotype they've studied to its origin, the groups it affects, and at least one example of someone who broke it. The collaborative canvas grows into a class-built critique of social pressure, grounding the unit's central inquiry in specific, named examples rather than abstract principles.

ci: Understanding global issues and using effective communication empowers us to raise awareness and inspire action.
lo: Global issues are caused by interconnected factors that require collective solutions (Causation) • Presentations in the media can reflect the perspective of the author rather than the truth (Perspective) • We must make responsible choices when responding to and creating media (Responsibility) • Messages can be expressed through a range of mediums and elements can be adjusted to enhance effectiveness (Communication) • Design choices contribute to the effectiveness of a message (Influence)

s.d: Students produce their PYP Exhibition media campaign film using green screen technology, combining scripted live performances with researched content about their chosen global issue, incorporating iMovie transitions and music transfers learned during production, and presenting alongside their keynote display on Exhibition sharing day.

Students produce an Exhibition podcast episode investigating their chosen global issue, structured using the MISO research method, incorporating bibliography sources curated with Katherine from the library, and drawing on insights from guest speaker Ben Lilley's media campaign expertise and the DLP-led upskilling sessions on podcast audio editing. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students compose original backing tracks and sound effects in GarageBand for their Exhibition live performance scripts, selecting instruments and layering audio to match the tone of their campaign message, then transferring the music to their iMovie Green Screen films as practised during the production phase.

Students build interactive 3D Exhibition displays in CoSpaces during the DLP upskilling sessions, creating virtual environments that present their global issue research and action plans using Canva AI vibe coding to generate interactive quizzes, games, or matching exercises that engage the Exhibition audience alongside their live performance component.

DLP captures 360° footage of the PYP Exhibition displays and live performances using the Insta360 camera, creating an immersive virtual gallery that preserves each group's keynote presentation, logo, campaign materials, and Green Screen film for families and the school community to revisit after Exhibition day.

ci: The way a country is governed impacts the lives of citizens.
lo: The key institutions and functions of Australia's democratic system of government (Function) • How government decisions impact citizens at federal, state and local levels (Causation) • The rights and responsibilities of Australian citizens and their obligations as global citizens (Equality/Citizenship)

s.d: Students produce a Canberra Tour documentary combining footage and reflections from visits to Parliament House, the High Court, Australian War Memorial, Museum of Australian Democracy, National Capital Exhibition, and the Embassy Tour, narrating how each institution connects to the three levels of government, the 4 Roles of Parliament, and the lines of inquiry explored throughout the unit.

Students produce a civics podcast episode debating a governance topic such as the Under-16 Social Media Ban, structured around the pros and cons brainstormed in table groups, incorporating vocabulary from the PEO 'What is Parliament?' video and the 4 Roles of Government lesson, and citizenship rights and responsibilities explored through the ClickView Australian Citizenship video and 'We Are Australian 2025' reading comprehension. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students investigate Australian drone regulations as a real-world civics case study, researching which level of government (federal CASA rules vs local council restrictions vs state laws) controls airspace, then programming CoDrone EDU flight paths within permitted boundaries, connecting to their exploration of how the three levels of government divide responsibilities and their Bill to Parliament planning.

Students design a 'Bill to Parliament' presentation outlining their proposed law inspired by issues they are passionate about, following the parliamentary process studied through the BTN 'Passing a Bill' video, including research evidence gathered using the Bill to Parliament PowerPoint planning template, and arguments structured from the pros and cons analysis practised during the Under-16 Social Media Ban debate.

Students use Minecraft Education to build a model of Australia's three levels of government as a physical landscape — federal institutions (Parliament House, High Court, Governor-General's residence) at the centre, state government buildings in a surrounding ring, and local council facilities (library, park, waste depot, community centre) in the outer zone. Roads and pathways between levels must represent the flow of decision-making: which laws travel from federal to state, which council decisions require state approval, where citizens interact with each level directly. After building, students introduce a policy challenge (a new school, a road, a community garden) and trace which level of government makes each decision, annotating the pathway through their built landscape. The Canberra Tour becomes a physical reference throughout the build.

Students explore the 'Civic Engagement and Citizenship' world — which follows real people making change through politics, community organising and protest — to analyse how ordinary citizens interact with democratic systems. They connect what they observe to the three levels of Australian government studied through the Canberra Tour, identifying where each form of civic action would be most effective. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/social-studies/Civic_Engagement_and_Citizenship

ci: The relationships between the Earth, Moon and Sun create predictable patterns that shape life on our planet and are understood through both scientific and cultural perspectives.
lo: The movement of the Earth, Moon and Sun causes predictable patterns such as day/night, moon phases, tides and seasons (Causation) • Different cultures, including Indigenous knowledge systems, interpret the night sky in unique and meaningful ways (Perspective) • The Earth, Moon and Sun have specific structures, positions and relationships that can be represented through models and simulations (Form)

s.d: Students design a 3D model in Tinkercad of their Moon phase tracking device incorporating a rotating display mechanism, with labelled sections showing each lunar phase from their Moon Diary observations — presenting their digital design alongside their micro:bit light sensor data and Lego Spike orrery to demonstrate how Earth, Moon and Sun relationships create predictable patterns.

Students produce a space science podcast selecting a topic from the free-choice board to research and investigate, modelling their episode on podcast features identified during the listening phase, covering concepts such as Earth's rotation causing day and night, the tilted axis creating seasons, Moon phases from their two-week Moon Diary, or Indigenous astronomy stories explored through the First Inventors resources. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students code an interactive space simulation in Scratch modelling how Earth's rotation causes day and night, how the tilted axis creates seasons across hemispheres, or how the Moon's orbit produces the sequence of phases tracked in their Moon Diary, building on the hands-on globe and flashlight experiments and Oreo cookie modelling completed during class lessons.

Students use Pico VR to explore immersive solar system environments, experiencing Earth's rotation, Moon phases tracked in their Moon Diary, and the scale of the Sun-Earth-Moon system up close, extending the hands-on globe and flashlight simulation into a 360° experience and connecting to the cultural astronomy perspectives from the First Inventors resources.

Students use Minecraft Education to build a navigable model of the Earth-Moon-Sun system — creating three scaled celestial bodies in accurate relative positions, with NPC characters stationed at each that explain its structure, movement, and relationship to the others when interacted with. Students program day/night cycles by rotating the Earth model on its axis and simulate moon phases by moving the Moon model around the Earth, checking their animations against the Moon Diary observations from class.
