The build externalises the unit's key challenge: understanding a three-body system requires holding multiple simultaneous movements in mind, and the act of programming those movements in Minecraft makes the relationships between rotation, revolution, and the patterns they produce physically concrete and testable.

ci: Peoples' actions affect the balance of nature.
lo: What the environment is comprised of and how ecosystems function (Form) • Our responsibility to care for and protect the natural world (Responsibility) • The interdependence between humans, animals and the environment (Interdependence) • The consequences of human actions on the balance of nature (Consequences)

s.d: Students build a prototype animal tracker using the micro:bit's accelerometer and radio features — mirroring the real technology conservation scientists use to monitor how animals like polar bears are being affected by climate change. Each student attaches a micro:bit to their wrist and programs it to transmit movement data via radio to a receiving micro:bit, detecting the difference between stillness and walking. They then discuss what a spike in readings might mean for a real tracked animal, and propose how the prototype could be adapted to monitor a locally threatened species — connecting the STEM Design Project's engineering lens to the unit's core inquiry into interdependence and the consequences of human actions on the balance of nature.

Students code an interactive 'Conservation Technology Showcase' in Scratch where visitors click on threatened species sprites to see animated demonstrations of how their STEM prototype addresses the threat — incorporating Dr Katherine Moseby's Plains Wanderer research data and the Borun and Tuk Dreamtime story connections to show how science and Indigenous knowledge work together for conservation.

Students produce a STEM Design Project documentary filming their prototype presentation and gallery walk, combining footage of their conservation technology model with narration explaining how the design addresses threats to their chosen species, referencing tracking and monitoring technologies observed at Melbourne Zoo and environmental observations from Camp Mallana and the Bairnsdale Keeping Place excursion.

Students build a Lego Spike prototype of their conservation tracking or monitoring device for the STEM Design Thinking Challenge, programming sensors and motors to simulate how their technology would detect and track their chosen threatened species, presenting the working model alongside their research PowerPoint during the gallery walk with 6A, 6B, and 6C.

DLP captures aerial footage of the Melbourne Zoo grounds and local conservation areas, providing a bird's-eye perspective of animal habitats and enclosures that students analyse alongside the Tracker Tech observations from their zoo excursion, adding spatial context to their STEM Design Project research on tracking and monitoring threatened species.

s.t: Minecraft Education (We Are the Rangers + Biodiversity)
s.d: Students use 'We Are the Rangers' to investigate how conservation technology protects threatened species — directly connecting to their STEM Design Project on animal tracking. They then explore 'Biodiversity' to travel through time, meet extinct species, and design solutions to biodiversity threats. The two worlds ground the unit's central ideas — interdependence, human responsibility and consequences — in immersive, evidence-based experiences. Access at education.minecraft.net/resources/climate-science/we-are-the-rangers and education.minecraft.net/resources/science-subject-kit/biodiversity

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human migration is a response to challenges, risks and opportunities.
lo: The causes for and consequences of migration (Causation) • The changes in societies as a result of migration (Change) • The impact of migration on the individual, both positive and negative (Impact) • Migration results in a more diverse society (Diversity)

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Migration Stories' experience in Scratch where visitors click on character sprites representing the migrants and refugees they interviewed to hear recorded excerpts from the 'This is Your Life' biography, with animated push and pull factor diagrams and journey maps — creating a digital exhibition that brings human migration stories to life through interactivity.

Students produce a podcast episode telling the migration story of the person they interviewed for the 'This is Your Life' biography — structured not as a retelling but as an analysis through the unit's four conceptual lenses: Causation (what drove the migration), Change (what transformed for this person), Impact (the personal costs and gains), and Diversity (what this person's arrival added to Australian society). The podcast format requires students to synthesise interview material into structured argument rather than narrative summary — a more rigorous academic skill. A curated selection of episodes is compiled into a class 'Migration Stories' podcast series that gives the human voices collected through interviews a permanent, shareable form. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students program a CoDrone EDU to trace the migration route of the person they interviewed for the 'This is Your Life' biography — flying from the country of origin to Melbourne, navigating the waypoints of their journey (refugee camp, transit country, arrival port). The drone encodes the human geography of migration: students must calculate distances, plan stages and adapt when routes are blocked, making the abstract push-pull factors from the unit's research into a physical, programmable experience of what migration actually requires.

Students use Pico VR to virtually visit countries and regions connected to the migration stories studied during the unit — the settings of Hamid's Story, Ahn Do's journey from Vietnam, and the homelands of their interview subjects — building empathy and geographic understanding before completing their This is Your Life Micro Lab presentations.

Students design their 'This is Your Life' migration presentation for the Micro Lab format, incorporating interview responses sorted into concept headings (Causation, Change, Impact, Diversity), the Define-Connect-Extend thinking routine from the tuning-in phase, and comparative analysis of similarities and differences identified when watching the Fariba and Fatima refugee video.

ci: Understanding ourselves, our relationships and our wellbeing empowers us to thrive.
lo: The recognisable features of safe and healthy interactions (Form) • The connections between emotions, behaviour, learning, interactions and goals (Connection) • The tools and skills to stay safe online and in person (Safety) • The active role we play in achieving and maintaining our wellbeing (Wellbeing) • The strategies to achieve and maintain positive relationships (Relationships)

s.d: Students code an interactive 'Wellbeing Toolkit Navigator' in Scratch where visitors select a challenge scenario (friendship conflict, online safety dilemma, emotional regulation, goal-setting) and the program guides them through the ROAR approach strategies — connecting CyberSafety golden rules, URStrong friendship tools, and Grow Your Mind techniques into one interactive resource Year 5 students can use.

Students produce a podcast episode advising their future Year 5 self — or a younger sibling starting high school — on navigating one of the key challenges explored during the unit: managing a digital relationship conflict, building resilience after a social setback, or setting and protecting personal boundaries online and in person. The advice must be grounded in the specific strategies from the CyberSafety lessons, URStrong friendship tools, and Grow Your Mind program — not generic reassurance. Recording advice for a real, named audience (rather than presenting to a teacher) forces students to make their learning genuinely transferable: the clarity of the explanation reveals the depth of the understanding. Episodes are preserved as a Year 6 wellbeing resource. The school has RODE Portal podcasting equipment available as a higher-quality recording alternative to Adobe Express — ideal for this task. When recording in Adobe Express, use the built-in background noise remover, which is highly effective at cleaning up classroom audio.

Students build an interactive 3D 'Who I Am at Wesley' virtual gallery in CoSpaces featuring their IB Learner Profile portrait drawing (half self-portrait, half strengths icons), their Camp Milana reflections, and their Canberra Tour learning — creating a walkable virtual exhibition where visitors experience how understanding ourselves empowers us to thrive.

Students use live polling during URStrong and CyberSafety sessions to share anonymous responses about friendship scenarios, online safety strategies, and wellbeing check-ins, building class data on which strategies from the Grow Your Mind and CyberSafety programs students find most effective for managing emotions and staying safe.

Students create an Adobe Express Character Animator piece voicing a scenario from the unit — choosing a character and recording a short monologue showing how they would handle a difficult online or in-person interaction, applying the wellbeing strategies and communication skills explored throughout the unit. The character animates automatically to their voice; they add a relevant background and music that reflects the emotional tone. Watching the class collection of animated scenarios creates a gallery of modelled safe and healthy interactions — far more memorable than a poster or written reflection.
